enhancement of prevention, diagnosis, and refinement of available, including innovative, treatments, as well as dissemination of information among States regarding achievements attained within their own borders, thereby increasing the level of understanding of the disease.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the situation facing the region, particularly the city of Naples, has been universally known for some time now, and unfortunately, it is well known to the entire world; the European Union has therefore had to address this issue on multiple occasions.

Faced with such a situation, now paradoxical in its third-world character, I therefore ask the Commission what consequences Italy will face from the standpoint of community law, and what economic sanctions might be imposed on Italy if the rural waste crisis fails to find a definitive solution in the near future.

I further request that the Commission provide, in detail, the data concerning the European funds that have so far been managed by the Campania Region for the establishment of an integrated waste collection and disposal system.
You see, Commissioner, usually when we speak of emergencies, we refer to dramatic, extreme situations that nevertheless find a solution within a reasonable timeframe.
When a so-called emergency lasts for more than 15 years, evidently something is not working.

In recent years, the Italian government has done everything possible to resolve the long-standing issue of waste collection and disposal, but evidently, if today Naples' streets are overrun by tons of waste,
tons of waste—blame primarily lies with the local Campanian authorities and their complete inability or lack of will to take action to find a solution.

It is clear and absolutely beyond any doubt that the conditions in which Naples and Campania find themselves are due to the long-standing neglect exhibited by municipalities, provinces, and regions in managing the waste cycle.
If differentiated waste collection is not properly carried out—the first indispensable element of a functioning waste management plan—it cannot be blamed on the central government.

If the Italian government had to deploy the army, this occurred a few weeks ago, but it has happened on other occasions previously, when hundreds of newly enlisted recruits suddenly and simultaneously fell ill; clearly, this cannot be the fault of the central government.

If the emergency has struck Naples, while conditions in the rest of Italy are very different—such as in my region, Veneto, where selective waste collection has been carried out for years without problems and with an extremely high recycling rate—it means that the responsibility for the emergency lies

on Campania's politics and local administrators, and especially on the serious complicity with organized crime that has long sought and obtained enormous profits from the waste management business, thanks to the Camorra's infiltration of politics and local administrations.
After the lies of the Berlusconi government, which took office in 2008 telling Italians that the waste problem had been overcome, it is right that the European Union
blocking funds to Italy until a sustainable waste cycle is submitted, based on waste recycling and composting.

And it is certainly not the plan presented by President Caldoro, which once again relies on uncontrolled landfills such as those in Chiayano, Taverna del Re, and Terzigno, even within the Vesuvius National Park, nor on incinerators that cause death, cancer, or a genuine public health emergency.
Public funds in Campania, during the environmental emergency of these thirteen years, have strengthened criminal ties between politics—both left and right—businessmen, and the Camorra.

and in environmental matters, dissent is resolved through the criminalization of opposition, through the criminalization of local communities, families, and the territory itself, as the Italian government has done by using the malganello to suppress those defending nature and the land.

International law defines a refugee as someone who, fearing persecution due to race, religion, nationality, social group membership, or political opinions, is outside the country of their nationality.
Cesare Battisti is not a political refugee.

Despite the protection France has offered for years, we are dealing with a multiple murderer, either the perpetrator or instigator of four killings, who has concealed himself behind the pretext of political struggle to evade life imprisonment.
It is shameful for a major country like Brazil to deny the extradition of a criminal who has never shown remorse, while granting him the status of political refugee.
Italian authorities, and above all the victims' families, demand to see this criminal brought to justice.
Faced with President Lula's insistence in denying extradition, the EU cannot leave the Italian government to face the situation alone.

Europe must and should use all diplomatic tools at its disposal to ensure Italy obtains the extradition of Battisti, even at the cost of threatening the suspension of cooperation agreements with Brazil.
provide concrete support to the Tunisian people and their transitional government, to revive an Eastern Mediterranean dialogue that has been strongly neglected in recent years.
The outburst of anger among the youth and the poor of the Maghreb and Egypt stems from the unjust distribution of wealth and the constraints on the exercise of fundamental freedoms.

In North Africa, people are rising up, demanding more democracy, more economic opportunity, greater participation, more jobs, and are looking with astonishment at the old European continent, frozen in place, unable to grasp the social transformations taking place in that region or to develop genuine policies for progress and peace.
Europe pays greater attention to human rights and democracy, promotes more dialogue, more cooperation, and more substantive economic interventions.
Europe must open its doors and its heart to North Africa in order to make the Mediterranean a sea of peace.
Enough with the rejection of immigrants and the violent cooperation with countries like Libya; more investments, more welcoming, more aid, more Euro-Mediterranean policy.
It is demanded by history.
The resolution adopted today by a majority on the waste emergency in Campania is a very important signal.
The Campania emergency becomes a European emergency, and it is a decision in favor of the citizens of Campania.
We say no to the allocation of funds that merely strengthen corrupt politicians, unscrupulous entrepreneurs, and the camorra; yes to European money if a credible plan is presented.

No to an environmentally compatible waste disposal system, no to uncontrolled landfills in national parks, no to incinerators producing dioxin, yes instead to recycling and environmentally sustainable practices.
and one also says no to the criminalization of local communities' dissent, thus yes to legality.

It is a significant turning point, a warning to the Berlusconi government and to the regional government led by Caldoro, which so far have done nothing but talk and entrench their cliques and the mafia-connected bourgeoisie.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the issue of counterfeit medicines is becoming an increasingly widespread problem on the international and community levels, both due to the rising number of cases and the growing sophistication of these counterfeits.
It is to these challenges that the report by the honourable Mattias seeks to respond, the result of long and painstaking negotiations.
the report to which we expressed our support has established a regulatory framework that, starting from more precise and detailed definitions, has taken into consideration
all stakeholders, stages, and withdrawals within the pharmaceutical supply chain, safety, traceability, distribution chain obligations, achieving a good balance.
These are some of the aspects on which efforts must be made in order to achieve the primary objective of establishing stricter standards.
of counterfeit medicines, leading to a positive and more comprehensive regulatory framework capable of safeguarding citizens' health and protecting them from the risk of serious consequences.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, from Egypt, from the virtual square of Facebook to the real square, with millions of people demanding a fairer distribution of wealth, greater justice, and protesting against corruption.

From the transition of soldiers and tanks, we must move toward genuine democracy, a new constitution, new elections, the abolition of the death penalty, more humane prisons, social rights, and freedoms.
It will be difficult.
Lurking in ambush are those who, in the name of an idea, believe they may employ any means.
We wish to believe in the marvelous Egyptian people, who have never however experienced democracy.

We want a secular democracy, with a clear separation between religious authority and political power, a democracy that integrates minorities and fulfills genuine rights—public health, social security, employment.
Europe should support this path, but by investing resources capable of fostering Egypt's genuine development—no more dictatorships, no more poverty, no more misery.
Mr. President, Commissioner, I believe that the issues concerning the Mediterranean, with this measure, are ushering in a phase even more pressing than the actions we have already taken.
I believe that the act we are launching today is far more important than the regulation of 2006 and its effects.

I believe this also addresses some limitations of that regulation, which, being unilateral, has often led fishing sectors to maintain the view that yes, Europe is doing its part, but the other riparian countries are not.
I believe it is strategic for us to address this topic in this dimension and with these characteristics.
The work carried out by our colleague Rivellini, which has been widely acknowledged, undoubtedly merits a formal acknowledgment here.
Nevertheless, I have the clear impression that the fact that we are unable to resolve this issue at first reading and will therefore proceed to the second reading
particularly regarding the interpretation of Article 28 of this regulation, raises a question. In this instance, the Parliament aligns itself with the Commission.

That is, the Parliament essentially states that it is appropriate for these delegated acts to be under the Commission's responsibility; however, there is some resistance from the Council, and today's absence is likewise significant.
But this act has fostered in Parliament the idea that preserving biodiversity and maintaining the fishery potential of this important sea is also the future of fishermen.
But having said that, I believe the phase ahead of us must centrally repropose the concept of co-decision.

These delegated acts have their own rationale for being delegated and are therefore entrusted to the Commission today, but international agreements must ensure that the Parliament's role remains firmly upheld when these take place.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, in my opinion we are facing two issues in this matter: one is methodological, the other concerns the substance.

The point of method is that we are proceeding through a device for which we thank the honourable Fraga, namely oral questions, in order to discuss something that should, instead, following the Treaty of Lisbon, be a right of this Parliament.
I believe this practice must end; we cannot resort to a strategy aimed at guiding a co-decision process.
Co-decision occurs when it is cultivated in the stages in which it matures.
Today we are holding this debate; the treaty ends, it will continue, and at the end we will say yes or no.
Well, this method needs to be reconverted.

On the substance, however, I merely wished to emphasize—agreeing with many points made by my colleagues—the assessment of the impacts of fishing agreements, so that they are not merely commercial deals through which we purchase cephalopods at one euro per kilogram in Mauritania.

These indicators must be capable of measuring not only sustainability, but above all the impact on the economy of that country, understanding how many jobs are affected, assessing the level of innovation in artisanal fishing, and evaluating the use of these resources.
not merely a straightforward commercial exchange, but a fishing agreement, with all its implications.
Mr. President, the favorable vote expressed on the honourable Rivasi's report stems from an assessment of the management of the H1N1 influenza.
2009001, which is 2009010, aims to establish general conduct criteria valid for any future occasions of global public health emergency.
The report, following a long and positive phase of revision, establishes cooperation, independence, and transparency as its core principles.
It calls for greater and improved cooperation between the health authorities of the Member States and European institutions, in order to establish a harmonized and common risk management approach.
The objectives are the assessment of Member States' strategies regarding national influenza management plans, including vaccine stockpiles, the publication of names of...
all experts consulted by European health authorities and an effective relationship of trust with media entrusted with the dissemination of public health messages.
That is why it received our favorable vote.

Mr. President, Mr. President, up to today, the recommendations adopted by the General Fisheries Commission for the Mediterranean have been incorporated into Community legislation provisionally, through annual fishing opportunities regulations.
These concern conservation, management, exploitation, control, and marketing measures for fisheries and aquaculture products in the Mediterranean.
The permanent nature of these recommendations, however, requires a more stable legal instrument for their incorporation into Community law.
It is therefore advisable to incorporate the aforementioned recommendations through a single legislative act, to which future recommendations may subsequently be added.
Today we have done this, and it will enhance legal certainty while simultaneously representing a significant step toward simplification.
At this point, the contracting parties to the CGMPM are obliged to ensure full implementation of the agreed measures from the date specified.
A timely transposition was therefore necessary in order to make these international measures directly applicable to natural or legal persons at the community level and to ensure legal certainty.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I believe the approval of our colleague Podimata's report represents a significant step forward toward a more balanced, more advanced, and stronger economic and fiscal policy in Europe.

It is now evident to all the urgency of equipping ourselves, as Europe, as a coordination among the twenty-seven countries, with economic and financial instruments capable of governing the economy, rather than merely submitting to it.
For this reason, the proposal to tax financial transactions has generated significant echo, extensive debate, and considerable attention.

The fact that the majority of this Parliament voted in favor of this provision is an important and significant development, as for the first time it did not merely let things pass, but instead took a step forward.
I hope the Commission and the Council continue along this path.

Madam President, I welcome the reports on gender equality and women's poverty in Europe, and I am pleased that this discussion is taking place precisely today, the centenary of International Women's Day.

Despite Directive 2002-73 on equal treatment between men and women in relation to access to employment, training, and promotion, as well as working conditions, further efforts are still required to ensure proper implementation.

Consider that, despite the significant increase in female education levels in recent years and the fact that the number of female graduates now exceeds that of male graduates, women remain concentrated in traditionally female-dominated sectors—often less remunerative—and hold fewer leadership positions across all spheres of society.

The lack of access to care services for dependent individuals, such as children, people with disabilities, and the elderly, along with inadequate parental leave schemes and flexible work arrangements, prevents women from working.

I would like to conclude by remembering the women around the world who are fighting for the most fundamental rights, and I would like our Assembly to express solidarity with Iranian feminist associations that are today taking to the streets, calling on women to demonstrate without the hijab.

As all the colleagues who have spoken have said, we are finally beginning to talk again, and I hope we will soon resume taking action as well, through targeted choices in economic and common industrial policy.
Thanks to the Lange report, the excellent work carried out by the colleague, and the communication from President Tajani.

This choice means several important things. First, as already stated, the revival and recovery of the European economy and the sustainability of employment also lie, and perhaps above all, in the recovery and support of the entire world.
manufacturing sector, to both more advanced and even traditional sectors, because there is a frontier that can still be tackled there, and this was not so obvious a few years ago.

Second, the recovery of industrial sectors in Europe also means that our competitiveness is no longer being decided in the fields of finance and services, but here, and it is not too late for us to rise to the challenge of Europe to regain competitiveness worldwide in industrial sectors.

Third point: It has been stated that the new industrial policy focuses on factors rather than sectors, emphasizing the factors and tools that can truly foster the emergence of new enterprises and enhance capacity for resilience and modernization.

Therefore, I believe it is important to highlight these new factors requiring action, as has been done—credit, in particular. We call for new initiatives, beyond those already announced, including those by the Commissioner on the issue of credit, raw materials, and energy costs.

innovation, research, the need to strengthen in Europe the capacity to transfer technological results from research and innovation into enterprises, into new products, and into new modes of organizing production.

We must recognize the value of a very distinctive fabric within our Europe, and particularly in certain countries—the fabric of SMEs, the quality of enterprises, the quality of products, in short, the distinctiveness of European industry.
And I believe we should rediscover these manufacturing traditions, these manufacturing roots, in many countries, in order to renew and transform them.
Finally, the issue of territory, which is very important.
European policies succeed when they intersect with and find a territory capable of addressing them.
Therefore, thanks to Lange, and we look forward to an excellent collaboration with the Commission to achieve concrete results in a short time.
Many milestones have been achieved, but some battles still lie ahead.
Today, the urgency concerns women from other cultures living in Europe and around the world, in contexts where it is difficult for them to claim minimum standards of dignity and humanity.

We cannot celebrate this festival without placing the issue of women in the Arab-Muslim world at the center; otherwise, we continue to ignore a problem that currently affects them but will soon become ours as well.
We cannot ignore the rising emergency figures that grow year after year, even in Europe, in our cities, in our schools where our children grow up.
For some time, cases of all forms of violence experienced by Muslim women within their own families have been increasing.

For this reason, I have long been calling for and I reaffirm today that Europe must implement targeted policies against the spread of Islamic radicalism, forced marriages, honor killings, female genital mutilation, and domestic violence.
We European men and women must fight for those women who live in Europe, but not only there, who have no voice and are oppressed by the monster of Islamic fundamentalism.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I voted in favor of the resolution on Turkey because the constitutional reform, which took place on December 12th, has been unanimously recognized as an important step taken by Turkey towards Europe.
Naturally, many steps still remain to be taken, and in this regard, I express concern over the numerous arrests of journalists.

I emphasize that Turkey is the seventh largest trading partner of the European Union, and that the European Union is Turkey's primary trading partner; furthermore, commercial exchanges, correctly interpreted over the centuries, have fostered mutual penetration and peaceful understanding among peoples.

I therefore applaud the significant progress made in enhancing connectivity between the European Union and Turkey, particularly through the maritime highways crossing the northern Adriatic and the northern Tyrrhenian.
on which now flows a traffic exceeding 250,000 trucks annually in both directions, connected to a significant railway truck-tier-sub-wagon link from port to inland European destinations.
In this way, a significant truck traffic on road has been eliminated, and I hope that progress continues along these lines.
Mr. President, with today's vote we have given further momentum toward a definitive solution to this issue, but we have not voted for the best possible solution.

Indeed, I would like to recall that the over 300 amendments submitted to the report have been consolidated into 38 compromise amendments, all adopted, which contribute to more clearly defining the priority areas of the strategy as well as the objectives of this strategy.

The Commission, through the permanent task force, shall ensure the collection and dissemination of statistical data and best practices, and Member States should appoint, we hope, a senior government official to serve as a contact point for the implementation of the strategy.
We could have been even more impactful and less hypocritical if, in the report, we had emphasized the obligations and duties that the Roma community should still uphold in host countries.
This is my hope, namely that solutions will soon be found that enable better coexistence of this community across all member states.
Lampedusa has been overrun by tens of thousands of North Africans, an influx the island could never possibly accommodate.
For weeks, Italy has been targeted by hundreds of balconies of undocumented immigrants.
And if we do not all react together, if the EU does not react, the wave of people fleeing will continue.
The irregular migrants who arrived in recent months must be sent home; there is no alternative.
But the Italian government, beyond a few words of solidarity, has been left alone to manage this biblical exodus of unforeseeable proportions. Where is Europe?
To date, Brussels has been conspicuously absent, displaying shameful conduct that my fellow citizens can no longer tolerate.
Europe has left Italy alone to face an unprecedented emergency.

Instead, Europe not only must assist Italy in repatriating undocumented migrants, but must also support the Italian government's efforts to prevent thousands upon thousands of people from continuing to depart, particularly from Tunisia.

A completely different matter, however, concerns the Libyan refugees, toward whom Europe must stand alongside Italy in clearing operations and logistical management, but above all must commit to ensuring that refugees are hosted across all member states.
The reception of those fleeing war cannot fall solely on Italy.
It is time to stop all this chatter.
The European Union, if it exists, should make its presence felt.
We cannot tolerate Italy being filled with undocumented immigrants while France rejects them using shameful methods.
The irregular migrants must be sent back to their home countries, while all member states must commit, from now on, to hosting Libyan refugees.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the issue of ensuring adequate financial resources to support the decommissioning processes of nuclear plants is a matter already addressed by

European Union legislation on nuclear safety is currently of great relevance, clearly in light of recent international developments and the resulting decisions taken by the governments of the Union on this matter.

The work of the honourable Marinescu, which we have expressed our support for, addresses the specific cases of certain countries that, at the time of their accession, undertook particular obligations regarding the decommissioning of nuclear facilities and which have received, precisely for this activity, special support from the European Union.
Beyond specific cases, it is imperative to establish effective verification and inspection controls over the nuclear power plants located within the territory of the Union.
Indeed, a high number of reactors are estimated to be shut down in the near future within the community territory.

It will therefore be essential to ensure guarantee mechanisms, also and especially in these cases, for the allocation of adequate financial resources necessary to meet both ordinary and extraordinary needs.
I would also like to thank the rapporteur.
The report addresses the recent developments in changes to control and communication systems.
For this reason, a picture of significant heterogeneity emerges, particularly among states in reporting irregularities and frauds.

The report repeatedly highlights the peculiar nature of the low levels of fraud and irregularities reported by various countries concerning agricultural expenditures, structural funds, and pre-accession funds.
Indeed, here we must clarify the statistical data that have been provided.
The situation is further exacerbated by the fact that the outcome stems from the ineffectiveness of recoveries.
However, one point is clear and confirms a long-term trend.

The fight for legality and transparency in the expenditure of European resources, as well as the struggle against the illusion and evasion of revenues, cannot yet rely on full cooperation from states, governments, and European institutions together.

In this sense, I consider timely the calls for revising the community legislation on public procurement and the need to establish what I refer to as a European public registry of beneficiaries of community funds.
I thank the rapporteurs, and also Commissioner Barnier for his great willingness towards the Parliament.
We support the idea of an internal market capable of encompassing and meeting social expectations and the social dimensions they entail.
It is not a utopia, it is realism and also modernity.

It is indeed this perspective that makes the market itself stronger, because the sole economic element—economicism, essentially the reduction of the market to economicism—has demonstrated that this approach fails to resolve either the problems of growth or those of cohesion.
In short, it means fully realizing, in the present, the entirely European concept of a social market economy.
In today's texts, this is present.
There is recognition of social enterprises, cooperatives, their role, mutuals, foundations—the ten percent of European enterprises and a large part of our employment.
They are realities that are genuine enterprises, demonstrating economic pluralism and enterprise pluralism.

They work with capital but not for capital, and they aim to achieve labor, welfare, quality, and social innovation, demonstrating that these values must and can be part of the internal market in Europe.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the justification provided by Beijing that the artist Ai Weiwei was arrested on economic charges is unconvincing; it is equally unconvincing that such relentless pressure is being exerted against a free and dissident artist.
It is unconvincing because such arrest is part of hundreds of arrests of opponents and the systematic repression of any dissent.

On the days when the Arab uprisings erupt, our thoughts go to the image of cruel violence in Tiananmen Square against young Chinese people who, as today's revolutionaries of the Jasmine Revolution demand, were calling for freedom and democracy.

China has opened up to the West economically, yet has failed to integrate production with environmental protection and workplace safety, and remains devoid of any opening toward democracy and pluralism.

Our action is not an interference in China's internal affairs, as Beijing claims, but rather an appeal by men and women, by movements, by parties committed to building a world free from intolerance on every continent.
The Beijing government should observe what is happening in the world to understand whether, if it does not change, it will be historical events and Chinese citizens who change China.
You're welcome.

This is the actual balance sheet in terms of impact on SMEs, under the Lisbon agenda's triumphant project and the Europe 2020 strategy, on which it is even being decided to concentrate the resources of the next multiannual financial framework, 2014–2020.

I can therefore only reiterate what thousands of European citizens and taxpayers are asking: what use is Europe if it is unable to resolve the continent's economic problems, cannot speak with one voice on foreign policy, and squanders money?
I hope that what Commissioner Tajani has stated on numerous occasions will be realized.
I hope the review of the SMEs Code is truly capable of simplifying bureaucracy, ensuring access to credit recovery, and facilitating digitalization.
With the directive on late payment, a concrete and tangible achievement has been reached, yet one resulting from political will rather than euro-propaganda.

If the costs of the latest strategy will still finance nothing but empty words, I urge European institutions to make amends toward the dozens of small entrepreneurs in my region, Veneto, who took their own lives before witnessing the collapse of their small businesses.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, while Europe pretends not to notice, the 2011 Europol report on organized crime states that with the accession of Romania and Bulgaria to Schengen, powerful Turkish, Russian, and Albanian criminal organizations will be able to expand their activities throughout Europe.
Moreover, verbatim, illegal immigration from Turkey will expand to the coasts of Bulgaria, and drug and human trafficking will increase in the hands of Turkish-Albanian crime.

Regarding human trafficking, Romania and Bulgaria's accession to Schengen could provide Bulgarian crime groups and the new Roma communities with new opportunities to expand their already substantial trafficking networks across the rest of Europe.
In short, increased illegal immigration from Turkey and expanded trafficking in drugs and human beings, less security for European citizens.
Europe wants to do a great favor to organized crime.
The Northern League party will always be opposed to this kind of reckless decision.
Anyone voting in favor of this report tomorrow will bear responsibility for a decision that will open a highway for organized crime straight to the heart of Europe.
Thanks to the Treaty of Amsterdam, environmental policy and sustainability policy have gained greater importance, with sustainability policy becoming even more significant.
Attention has focused on the integration between economic and environmental policies and on incorporating environmental issues into policies and other sectors.

Among the key areas are climate change, sustainable transport, nature and biodiversity, health and the environment, as well as the use of natural resources and waste management, and the international dimension of sustainable development.
In June 2006, the European Council, in its conclusions, invited the Union and its Member States to extend national accounts to encompass the fundamental aspects of sustainable development.
National accounts must therefore be complemented by an integrated economic and environmental accounting system, capable of providing fully coherent data.
I voted in favor of this regulation proposal because it establishes new uses for national accounting data, environmental statistics, and statistics in other statistical sectors.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, my favourable vote on the Garriga report on the Union's forthcoming financial perspectives stems from the conviction that precisely at a time of greatest crisis
that the so-called European spirit is decisive lies in insisting upon and achieving the necessity of providing credible tools—starting with adequate financial resources—to the Union.
Therefore, requesting an additional five for the next budget is a just and necessary request, perhaps not sufficient, but indispensable in order to provide answers to the major challenges before us.
tackle real challenges such as stronger cohesion among regions, greater research and innovation capacity, and a stronger ability to generate growth and provide employment.

adequate, none of these problems will find a sufficient solution, starting with the introduction of this intermediate category into cohesion policy, which only makes sense if it does not cause harm to the other existing categories.
This is why I voted in favor, and I hope the Commission and the Council will follow the guidance provided by this report.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the new Hungarian Constitution reduces the ability of oversight bodies, such as the Constitutional Court, to intervene and increases the powers of the executive, thus undermining the normal balance of powers.
Social rights are being eroded; the state provides subsidies to the needy only if they agree to perform public service work.
The right to self-defense is further established, allowing individuals to possess weapons at home even without a license.
The right to abortion is prohibited, and strongly discriminatory rules are introduced against homosexuals.
All of this is contrary to Directive 78/2000, which establishes a general framework for combating discrimination.

I therefore ask the Commissioner how she intends to act, given that the fundamental rights established by the new Hungarian Constitution are only marginally compatible with those of the European Union and the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.
We are about to vote on a strategic document of great significance that provides a solid framework for the negotiations on the financial perspectives beyond 2020.

Starting from the requirement of coherence between objectives and instruments, between the new role that European institutions are called upon to play after Lisbon and in the face of recession, and the resources we jointly decide to invest.
This coherence also implies a serious response to the demand for innovation emerging from public opinion, EU policy beneficiaries, and European social and productive forces.
We did so for cohesion, a crucial chapter in determining what we want and what the European Union should be in the coming years.
It tells us that cohesion is today a vital pivot of the European project, an important heritage for all to safeguard, protect, and renew.

Particularly critical on this point is the effort made regarding the assumption of introducing an intermediate category of regions, especially since in various cases the years of recession we have experienced in the past have
Widened internal disparities, making even more crucial efficient and adequate support for Europe's less developed regions, while combating waste and clearly outlining the development policy.
However, I believe the challenge posed by intermediate categories with the safeguard clause is one we must accept in order to usher in a new phase in politics.
of cohesion in Europe, and also to strengthen the cohesion policy with the more balanced and sustainable development that we aim to ensure for Europe in the coming years.
I also join in thanking the honourable Mr. Langen and the other colleagues with whom we have worked to finalize this complex report.
I believe it is particularly important what the honourable Member has recalled.
Langen, when you said that we wish to maintain the purpose and scope of this regulation, in keeping with the mandate given by the G20 in Pittsburgh in 2009.
The fundamental objective is to bring the majority of over-the-counter derivatives onto regulated platforms in order to avoid remaining exposed to a high degree of

risk and speculation. When, as has occurred, transactions and insurance involving four hundred million dollars take place without collateral, that is, without adequate reserves, one understands the risks we are running.

That said, I would also like to briefly mention two issues: the first concerns our relationship with the Council, and the second relates to the coherence of the proposal for the reform of financial markets that we must advance.

I raise the first issue regarding the Council, and I say this very clearly: the feeling is all too often that the Council acts as an obstacle to swiftly and effectively advancing financial markets reform.
I hope the new Presidency will take up this issue, because once again we must place in
I note an extension of the timelines, which is largely due to the positions, difficulties, and conflicts that emerge during Council sessions.
It is a problem that also affects the procedure we wish to follow.
The second issue concerns the consistency and specificity of the measures.
There is not only the measure concerning OTC, but also the measure on short selling and others.

I would like to highlight two specific issues: the issue concerning the commodity markets, a particular market requiring specific anti-speculative measures, and the problem of local authorities' exposure to derivatives in European Union countries.
I believe these are issues on which the Parliament must act.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the Fukushima incident continues to produce very serious consequences, with the release of radioactive seawater and water exceeding legal limits by up to five hundred times.
The risk that food products become contaminated is high.
Europe has established very strict controls, but these apply exclusively to fresh products.
No protective regulations have been issued for processed products, in particular for canned fish.
We import 370,000 metric tons of canned tuna, and the main suppliers are countries in Southeast Asia, which import fish from Japan.
This means that canned fish imported into Europe could contain Japanese fish.
It is fundamental and urgent to provide accurate information regarding the place of origin.
Today, consumers know where the box was produced, but not where the fish was caught.

The Commission has recently acknowledged this issue and intends to address the gap through the reform of the common fisheries policy, which will not enter into force before two years, just as the Sommer report, currently under discussion in this plenary, will not take effect before three years.
We must not create alarmism, but consumers have the right to urgent and transparent information.
The Commission must act as soon as possible.
Mr. President Tusk, your speech gives us good hope; the hope is that the Polish Presidency will be a contribution to the fight against an Europe of selfishness.
nationalist, without a vision for the future, which could be a contribution in favor of an Europe that truly sees itself as an integrated reality with a shared destiny.
But, as You rightly stated in Your intervention, a Presidency faces concrete problems to address and resolve.
In this brief time, I would like to highlight two crucial issues regarding the economic and financial crisis.
First issue: the reform of financial markets.
This is not a technical issue; it is a major democratic challenge of our time. The timelines are far too long; we must resolve matters such as the reform of short selling and OTC derivatives.
Second point: we are faced with the issue of taxing financial transactions; we must address it substantively, and we expect a concrete contribution from the Polish Presidency in this regard.
More than twenty years have passed since the signing of the Schengen Agreement.
No one questions the benefits of free circulation, but we must acknowledge that the current framework is no longer adequate to meet present and future needs.

For this reason, Member States and the EU as a whole must commit to reviewing the operational mechanisms of Schengen, so that freedom of movement does not come at the expense of our citizens' legitimate need for security.

To the extent of our current knowledge, the Commission's guidelines may also be partially acceptable, particularly regarding the introduction of a mechanism allowing Member States to temporarily restore border controls in the event of an emergency.
It must be seen, however, how this will happen and whether statements will be followed by actions, particularly by concrete results.

A comprehensive reform of the European border protection system will certainly also be required, particularly concerning external borders, where Frontex must become as soon as possible an agency with genuine operational authority and the capacity to support national authorities.
We must protect our external borders if we wish to continue with Schengen.
It is time for courageous choices in this Europe if one does not wish to merely survive.

The crisis has profoundly affected the everyday lives of European citizens and businesses, and risks, if we fail to respond, lowering irreversibly the social and cultural quality, well-being, and future prospects of young people and Europeans.

Far uscire la crisi from the crisis means addressing the identified causes with remedies, remedies against speculation, to which, if necessary, we must even cut the claws, imposing strict rules on financial markets.

remedies against recession, remedies against an imbalanced model of development that has placed profit above the person, remedies against the resurgent nationalism that prevents Europe from making the choices that rightfully belong to it.

Above all, it is time to define the tools, resources—even new ones—to move forward, to ignite the engines of growth through research, innovation, training, and support for productive investments, infrastructure, and employment, because without work, people lack dignity and rights.
This report, thanks to Pervance Beres and her colleagues, outlines a possible strategy for a proud Europe fully aware of its role.
to European leadership, if any exists, the responsibility now falls to implement it.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, behind the widespread prosperity of our continent lies a hidden Europe, often invisible, which we discuss in terms of numbers, statistics, and sociology rather than as real people—often women and children, or the elderly, that is, the most vulnerable population.
But Europe will not be credible to its citizens unless it learns to be consistent, and we here wish to be consistent in this Parliament, at this stage in Europe's life.

If we claim to base the Union on values, on human rights, I do not believe we can so drastically cut or terminate a program for those who do not eat, for those who lack the right to food, to access to food.

Fundamental, for an unresolved legal issue, is an agricultural fund, a social fund; and if it is to be social, Madam Commissioner, let it not come at the expense of other social programs, but rather be a new, specific fund, with appropriate resources.
We claim to want to ensure food security for Europeans, but the first right to security is the right to have food, which then should also be healthy.
Then here is our consistency and our commitment.
Thus, a legal solution must be found that ensures continuity and, with the support of non-governmental organizations and volunteers, guarantees the fundamental human right to food.
Mr. President, I am speaking solely to support this report and to highlight two particular aspects: one because it fits within the ongoing debate currently taking place on
revision of the common fisheries policy and the underlying option, which is the regionalization of a policy that in recent years, having been centralized, has demonstrated all its weaknesses.
This phase of regionalization finds a particular application in the Black Sea.
I believe the elements outlined in colleague Iotova's report describe a specific situation, yet one that could also occur elsewhere in Europe—there is no different situation in the Adriatic Sea.
of the Tyrrhenian or the Aegean Sea, that is, interactions—especially between European and extra-European fishing—are often highly damaging to European markets and European consumers.
The second issue I would like to briefly highlight is the reference to a common coast guard.
There is no politics without an exercise of oversight.

Today, controls are carried out through disparate forces across Europe. I believe it is time to consider how one of Europe's primary law enforcement agencies—the coast guards—could achieve common management, greater coordination, and, why not, a unified European force.
With this hope, I fully support the contents of my colleague Iotova's report.
Thank you, Mr. President. The disaster that struck the Gulf of Mexico over a year ago has prompted the European Commission to reflect on legislation concerning offshore oil platform activities.
The European Parliament, with this report, has also wished to express its position on the matter, adopting a text that requires appropriate technical and financial capabilities.
aimed at sector operators and designed to establish high safety standards, thereby reinforcing the European Union's leading role in promoting the safety of offshore activities.
The new interventions requested of the Commission should focus on international coordination and on a realistic approach that combines all existing strengths
in the sector at the level of procedures and avoid the creation of new bodies or additional bureaucracy that might jeopardize the achievement of concrete final objectives.

The report, in concrete terms, calls not only for greater accountability on the part of operating companies but also for improved mechanisms of communication and exchange of information and knowledge, and expresses the hope for a strengthening and expansion of the role and competences of the European Maritime Safety Agency.
For all these reasons, I consider the outcome achieved to be positive, thanks to the negotiation on this text, to which we have expressed our support.
I thank my colleagues Zeber and Schilakakis, and I also thank Commissioner Edegard.

I believe we are gathered here today to truly discuss and deliberate in order to achieve a robust European climate policy, capable of safeguarding economic development and the well-being of European citizens.
International climate policies, as has been recalled so far, have primarily focused on reducing CO2 emissions.
The proposal highlights how we must also address the fight against other anthropogenic emissions that contribute to the formation of tropospheric ozone, thereby impacting climate change.

Many things have already been mentioned, which I believe it is important to keep clearly in mind: today we are capable of doing so at absolutely reasonable costs, we have the technologies available, and although it is undeniable that CO2 emissions are primarily responsible

While it is undeniable that the other greenhouse gases present in the atmosphere are present in much smaller percentages, it is equally undeniable that their potential is very high, and thus we could indeed achieve significant results within just a few years, within just a few decades.
important.

There are warning signs; as the Commissioner also reminded us, even the United States has them. I have seen a study published in Nature by the scientific agency of the American Department of Commerce, which I find...

I believe there are many points of overlap with the analysis provided by our colleagues, and thus I imagine these could also be elements that enable Europe to present itself in Durban with a stronger, more concrete, and more significant position.

I conclude only by recalling, as already recalled, that in this exceptionally difficult and delicate moment we are experiencing, and as we have just spoken of the homeless only moments ago, there is a sensitivity among European citizens that we must take into account; therefore, considering that the great...

Regarding sensitivity, obviously, the top priority, and regarding jobs, we should be able to run a communication campaign that clearly explains to citizens what we concretely aim to achieve in terms of development, their well-being, and the protection of jobs.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the single market, free movement, and the Treaty of Lisbon are all elements that have come into effect independently of any change in our way of legislating, whether European or national.

Follow-up is required on the commitments arising from these occasions; in particular, a genuine phase of simplification of the legislative system must be launched—a phase that must foster a different dialogue between Europe and the individual member states.

I would start precisely with our documents; I take this opportunity to suggest a kind of clarity test for our documents. When we produce a white paper or a green paper, everything must be more comprehensible.

This virtuous phase of initiating legislation should be that of assigning a specific mandate to a European commissioner whose sole responsibility is to clarify legislation and the relationships between the member states and Europe itself.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, Mr. Representative of the Council, and Madam Commissioner, I too would like, as other colleagues have already done, to positively welcome the anti-corruption package adopted by the European Commission on 6 June.
Corruption is a transnational phenomenon, and combating it requires European-scale measures.
The vast majority of our European citizens, according to Eurobarometer estimates, believe that Europe should do more.
How could one blame them?

In many Member States, there has been a lack of political will to combat corruption—just consider that there are still European countries that have not signed the United Nations Convention against Corruption.

When considering the devastating impact of corruption on democratic processes, citizens' rights, and trust in the rule of law, it is clear that the national anti-corruption measures adopted so far by Member States are insufficient to contain a phenomenon that is expanding.

It is the European Union that must provide a united and robust response, especially now, amidst an unprecedented economic crisis weighing heavily on people's lives, the sustainability of welfare systems, and the capacity of institutions to ensure essential services.
Corruption causes the loss of 120 billion euros annually, equivalent to 1% of the European Union's GDP, and daily diverts resources from the Union and its member states that
They could be used to provide essential services—education, childhood support, employment, transportation—essentially diverting clean resources away from supporting people.

Therefore, greater courage and firmness are required, both in monitoring the implementation of anti-corruption measures and in intensifying and strengthening anti-corruption measures at the level of the Union.

In particular, we cannot speak of fighting corruption if we are unable to adopt at least minimum standards for defining the crime of corruption, a provision that is entirely absent from the recently adopted package.
The same applies to the definition of uniform parameters for measuring the extent of the phenomenon across member countries, which is essential for preparing an effective response.
We support, for example, revitalizing the EU network of contact points against corruption, as proposed in the resolution we will vote on tomorrow.
The crisis is not of yesterday; it has already existed for several years.
Perhaps it is now time to conduct a critical assessment of the policies pursued so far. Was everything always done correctly?
We have always carried out appropriate analyses of the situation.
the correct approach has been chosen
The same European Central Bank, which—as Minister Rostovsky stated—has saved Europe, has always acted appropriately, even when, fearing inflation, it raised interest rates?
President Barroso identifies a problem: the speed of markets and the slowness of politics. But is this the only problem?

Of course, if one considers that we are still at the implementation stage of July 21, this should at least suggest the need to better prepare and organize summits and their outcomes; however, politics will always be slower than markets.
The point, then, is to look further ahead, shifting the emphasis to the fight against unemployment and development.

This is the ground that the European Commission must choose. Even today, in President Barroso's speech, there was greater concreteness regarding the deficit and less clarity on growth and development guidelines.

The Commission must go beyond its weakness and state that the priority must be combating unemployment and creating jobs, redistributing wealth, and implementing a public investment strategy, while also changing the rules of the new European Stability Pact.
Mr. President, I believe the challenge of the coming years is to ensure that Europe, as has been stated, maintains its position of leadership.
a world-class tourist destination and, at the same time, capable of enhancing, both in terms of environmental preservation and competitive capacity, its entire territorial heritage.

It is a challenging and far from guaranteed endeavor, especially considering not only the opportunities but also the aggressiveness of other countries in this sector, from other continents, ranging from China to India to Brazil.
Now, in this context, as recalled by Commissioner Tajani, it becomes essential, as also noted in the opinion of the committee that we adopted in the Regional Development Committee,
to combine a strong overall strategy for tourism across the European Union with an appreciation of the role of regions and territories.
In this sense, full involvement from the outset is essential, in respect of the principle of subsidiarity of regions and local authorities.
Moreover, I believe that the development of integrated tourism networks and projects should be encouraged, starting with the establishment of partnerships for the exchange of best practices.
It seems appropriate, too, to propose establishing a Europe brand capable of defining high quality in the tourism product.
Of course, it will be up to the Commission to further define the priorities, particularly from a financial standpoint, to ensure that Europe's leadership is maintained in the future.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the preparation of a common strategic framework for funding research and innovation represents one of the greatest opportunities that European development and the European economy have before them.

It is indeed about acting on the lever that can truly trigger a moment of growth and also of new qualified employment, such as that offered to researchers and young people dedicated to new opportunities in innovation, technology, and related professions.
One observation, above all, emerges from this work: we must increasingly emphasize in Europe research and innovation that lead to practical application.
give rise to new enterprises that lead to new patents and do not remain merely basic research—valuable knowledge, indeed, but not sufficient to truly trigger the engine of development.

The second observation concerns the relationship with local authorities, regions, and territories, which also represent an important instrument for territorial programming and for fostering collaboration among research institutions, universities, centers of excellence, and businesses.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, European schools were established with the objective of ensuring access to education in one's mother tongue for the children of European Union officials called upon to work and live abroad in a cultural environment different from that of their home country.
The first European school was established in Luxembourg in 1953, initiated by a group of officials.
Over the years, considering the positive outcomes of this teaching approach, a total of fourteen European Schools have been established within the various Member States.

While acknowledging that the European schools system represents a necessity rather than a luxury, it is evident that, more than fifty years after the establishment of the first European school, this system may exhibit outdated elements and must therefore be appropriately adjusted and adapted to meet new economic and social needs.

I therefore express my favourable vote on the text submitted by colleague Cavada, which aims at greater involvement by the European Union to support and coordinate the actions of Member States, called upon to work towards developing and improving the system of European schools.
It is well known that investing in education means investing in the future of our European youth.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, in recent decades, there has been an exponential increase in the number of natural disasters causing the deaths of tens of thousands of people across various parts of the world.
Last year, within the Development Committee, I followed the work on the report concerning the European Union's capacity to respond to disasters.
It became clear how much still needed to be done to ensure a coordinated response by the European Union in the face of the increasing frequency of earthquakes, floods, and tsunamis.
The European Union's response to the Haiti earthquake, for example, clearly demonstrated the complexity of the existing tools and procedures.
Although Member States and Community institutions acted promptly in providing humanitarian aid, crisis management has raised a number of issues concerning efficiency, coordination, and transparency.
Precisely for this reason, I voted in favor of the text submitted by my colleague Elisabetta Gardini, which aims at greater efficiency in all phases of disaster management and improved coordination.
among the involved parties and a simplification aimed at avoiding unnecessary overlap and time losses that might hinder the provision of rapid and effective assistance.
It is merely a good initiative; in my opinion, we will instead need to develop a European policy regarding tourism.
The European label is a good idea, but I would like to focus on two points. There is no seaside or coastal tourism without a coastal planning policy.

I believe the time has come for Europe, precisely in order to safeguard this important sector, to launch an initiative in which the conception of the sea and of the coasts is not limited to a kind of urban planning that ends at the shoreline and fails to encompass the sea as a whole.
I believe that an integrated policy in this sense would be useful, as well as the need to develop everything that lies behind the claims made about rural tourism. Rural tourism can be
an excellent source of income for disadvantaged areas, for regions where employment no longer exists, where it is crucial not to continue with depopulation and instead ensure human settlement.

Well, there, mere statements are not enough; instead, Europe and individual countries should be encouraged to act through fiscal levers. Promoting tourism in rural areas could mean paying lower taxes; perhaps only in this way can a genuine opportunity truly be created for both tourism and the people living there.
Thank you, Mr. President. I would like to express my congratulations on the work carried out by Member Fidanza, which fits logically within the framework of the actions undertaken.
undertaken and promoted at the community level in the field of tourism, following the Treaty of Lisbon, which granted the EU new competences in this sector.
I agree and wish to emphasize the statement regarding the importance of a Europe that serves as a unified destination for certified, high-quality tourism, and the commitment to promoting this vision.
