enterprises, attention to the protection and enhancement of the environment proves capable of developing and thus responding to the main challenges posed by current circumstances.
For all these reasons, I consider this report positive, an important signal in the direction of promoting a European policy for tourism.
Mr. President, even for recovery, three small considerations: we need more Europe here too.
I believe it is inevitable that on certain matters there is truly an approach in which not only is a greater Europe hoped for, but actively built.
More Europe means, for example, genuinely beginning to consider a common road police force and identical rules regarding the protection of safety.
More Europe means having a European training policy, rather than simply delegating this to individual states, but instead building, for example, a genuine training policy.
towards schools, towards young people, and also towards categories somewhat more at risk—just mentioned a moment ago, motorcyclists, but truck drivers could also be mentioned.
More Europe means having common tools within the machines that improve safety conditions.
I believe they are a targeted strategy to be developed in collaboration with automobile manufacturers and with European vehicle production.
Here, I believe that if all this falls within the framework of a shared political commitment, I truly think the conditions might improve.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, this report addresses a subject that is global food security. "Global" means that this issue is not merely about world hunger in the poorest countries, but that food supply concerns affect all communities, including those in the most developed nations.

it outlines some key lines, such as the essential coherence between European policies and this objective of global food security, because we are an institution that preaches very well but is often not consistent in its choices.
I merely wish to outline three additional innovative themes that I believe we should develop further.
The first risk to food supply today stems from price volatility caused by speculation on food products, which must be contained or even prevented, among other factors.
Second, the issue of land grabbing must, in my view, be addressed through some form of treaty or negotiation, as it threatens not only the survival of many peoples but also democracy itself.
Third, the coherence between supply and energy policies.
We cannot, in order to produce new energy, prevent food from being produced for the world's populations.
I have voted in favor of this report by Member Zimmer, first and foremost for a reason of
a purely political nature, because I believe it is necessary to bring to the forefront of the European Union's entire initiative this major issue of food security.

It is not merely a matter of helping developing countries address this issue more effectively, but also the food crisis, given that demand is expected to increase by more than seventy percent by 2050.

The issue of agricultural products is a major concern that, if not addressed promptly and decisively, will become one of the main threats—perhaps the greatest threat—to stability, peace, and even democracy, as has already been demonstrated.

For this reason, I believe it is essential that the entire European Union, together with its member states, make every necessary effort—starting with appropriate economic investments, as highlighted in the report—toward addressing this major issue seriously.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, greetings to the Commission. I must truly thank all those who have contributed over these past weeks and months to the creation of this document which we are voting on today in plenary.
We have worked, I must say, very effectively, and a truly constructive debate has emerged.
The resolution proposal we are voting on today is the result of truly collaborative work and reflects the contribution of all political forces and the entire Parliament.
I believe a very strong collective sensitivity has developed regarding this issue, especially because we must remember that we live in an era truly marked by an exponential increase in disasters.
The numbers, if we look at them in their raw form, are very telling. The number of disasters worldwide has quintupled, considering that in 1975
Seventy-eight catastrophic events had been recorded, whereas today we face nearly four hundred disastrous events per year.

Over the past twenty years, unfortunately, the victims of these disasters have numbered more than ninety thousand, while over twenty-nine million people have been affected by disasters, with economic losses amounting to two hundred and ten billion euros.
It is clear that we are facing phenomena of such magnitude that demand a stronger response, a more effective response, and a truly strong commitment on our part.
We all have in our minds the images of the earthquake in Haiti, the floods in Pakistan, and what occurred in Japan.
Europe itself witnesses sudden floods and wildfires on its territory each year; we recall the ash cloud following the eruption of the Icelandic volcano.
There is no need to go further to affirm how important the work being carried out here in Parliament is, guided by two principles.
fundamental ones, which are our guiding stars—the principle of solidarity, reinforced by the solidarity clause of the Treaty of Lisbon, and the principle of subsidiarity.
We move along these lines.

The European Union's intervention has always been generous and effective in the past, as I believe is demonstrated by the tireless work of our Commissioner Gheorgheva; however, today we need to make a qualitative leap.
A leap in quality that we are ready to make, moving from an ad hoc response to one where the reaction
be strengthened through the early identification of existing resources in Member States made available to the European Civil Protection Mechanism.
It is important to emphasize that for this qualitative leap and for moving from information sharing and emergency response to the role of planning and monitoring.

the coordination we hope for, the fundamental role of services such as GMS, and I take this opportunity to also express the concern regarding what is happening with the budget for these new technologies, which are essential.
We have emphasized the importance of communication with citizens, as the Japanese experience has taught us this as well—this is never sufficient and never enough.
and we await from the Commission, which we urge to present as soon as possible a legislative proposal on civil protection.
Ethnic minorities oppressed, Christians forced to secretly live their faith, priests brutally killed while authorities remain inactive—this is today's Turkey.
My party, the Northern League, has consistently opposed Turkey's entry into Europe, as it represents a potential Trojan horse for radical Islamism on our continent.
Today more than ever, we are convinced that the EU must put an end to the accession negotiations.
The intolerable threats that Turkey has made against Europe must receive an immediate response.
One cannot accept that a third country dictate to Europe what it must do.
Cyprus is a newly acceded member of the EU and has an unquestionable right to assume the rotating presidency.
Europe must send a clear signal and demonstrate its readiness to defend Cyprus with every available means against Turkey's political and military arrogance and aggression.
We immediately halt the accession negotiations and remain prepared to suspend commercial relations as well.
Mr. President, we are experiencing a decisive turning point for the future of the euro and, with it, for the entire European project.
Having this awareness would mean moving beyond a myopic logic that subordinates everything to an ideological insistence on budgetary balance.
We all want the accounts of various states to be in order, and we all want to reduce indebtedness.
However, it is clear that without an intelligent investment policy capable of revitalizing the economy, there will be no possibility of growth.
The only clear outcome in this setup is that heavier prices, as always, will be borne by the most vulnerable social classes.
For this reason, together with the group of Socialists and Democrats, we voted against this report.
Indeed, one continues in a single direction, that of rigor without growth, a direction that has proven incorrect thus far.
I greatly appreciated the work that colleague Ferrera has done on this report.
Indeed, regarding the package of six legislative proposals concerning economic governance, only on this one did I and my group cast a positive vote.

We did so because, among other things, a clause safeguarding social rights through collective bargaining has been included, and furthermore, the role of Parliament in adopting delegated acts has been strengthened.

Thus, although we cannot alter the overall balance of the package of six proposals, this report certainly represents a step forward, among other things, toward a surveillance system that takes fundamental rights into account.

And thus, in this context, while maintaining that, in our view, a perspective based solely on an austerity policy does not yield positive results, this report undoubtedly represents a positive note contrary to the prevailing trend.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, Finland and the Netherlands have raised this issue in Council for a very simple reason: Bulgaria and Romania are currently unable to ensure effective border control, and are plagued by rampant corruption involving both the political class and organized crime.
Thinking about it this way, it is not the political parties and populist movements, but Europol in its 2010 report on organized crime.
There is no populist propaganda, but a serious security issue.

Should Bulgaria join the Schengen area at this moment, police and security agencies anticipate that Bulgarian borders—particularly the maritime ones—would become an easy target for Turkish and Eastern European crime, and that Bulgaria and Romania would become a crossroads for human and drug trafficking.

It is natural that some Member States have considerable reservations about Bulgaria and Romania's actual ability to ensure effective border controls against organized crime and corruption, and it is therefore entirely natural that they have opposed their accession to Schengen.
Allowing this now would pave the way for transnational crime and create a breach in Europe's borders, precisely at a time when there is intense migratory pressure from Turkey.
The reasons are many and opposing, and Europe cannot ignore them.
I therefore wish to know the intentions of the Commission and the Council regarding this delicate matter, which in my opinion has so far been addressed with...
sufficiency and without taking into account the risks that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania would entail, according to competent bodies dealing with security and international crime.

In my view, the Commission and the Council should postpone the accession of these two countries to Schengen and grant approval only when there is reasonable assurance that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania will not affect the security of European citizens.

Mr. President, the crisis in Europe is entangling public finances and private capital issues in a vicious cycle with the productivity challenges facing our enterprises, and the prospect of recession is real.
If therefore the priority given to SMEs is crucial, I call on the Commission to ensure, together with the Parliament, that our sound principles are actually put into practice.
practice, and that the legislative initiatives of the SBA, the Small Business Act, become concrete practice among the Member States, with a high level of effectiveness and quality.

Mr. Commissioner, consider what would not be possible if all Member States accelerated the implementation of the directive on payment delays? What a significant advantage this would be for SMEs! Therefore, SME evidence is important, but we are still at the methodological level.

It is certainly right to verify whether, when we legislate, we are truly doing so in the interest of and with due consideration for small and medium-sized enterprises; however, we urge that progress be made with great consistency and tenacity in thoroughly developing the framework of opportunities you have outlined.
We appreciate the efforts being made also by the Commission, and we are ready to support it, through the budget and other means at our disposal.
I will say only one word about credit.
Let's have a clear conversation with the banks, I say this to you, Commissioner, because we must support small and medium-sized enterprises.
Mr. President, I have expressed my favorable vote on the text drafted by my esteemed colleague, Ms. Wallis.
without prejudicing the usual avenues of access to justice, I am convinced that an alternative dispute resolution (ADR) offers a swift and cost-effective alternative to legal litigation.
In addition to being an effective tool for small and medium-sized enterprises, ADR can also see significant development online for the resolution of minor disputes.

Therefore, through joint action with the Member States, it is necessary to foster the creation of a harmonized legal framework that, by strengthening citizens' trust in the internal market, can contribute to stimulating the European economy.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the report on the provisions for sectors placed on the market under the flexibility regime, which we have voted in favour of, has been guided by a measured and realistic approach that has

concretized, on the one hand, by avoiding setting unrealistic and unattainable requirements, and on the other hand, by establishing a so-called flexibility regime, structured through the provision of time periods and corresponding quantitative limits of

The permitted emissions scheme provides for the annual release onto the market of a predetermined share of tractors meeting specific characteristics, thus enabling a gradual alignment with set standards over time, without causing disruptions to the affected production and industrial sectors.

Honourable Tattarella, the work you have carried out has been significant and has successfully achieved a careful balance between the demands arising from the major challenges facing the European Union at this time and those related to environmental protection and the current economic crisis.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the current global economic crisis, even more severe than that of 1929, has caused unsustainable imbalances in international economies over the past years.
Commercial imbalances among countries, financial markets lacking common rules, and instability in banking systems are all potential threats to financial and economic stability.
This resolution, which I express my support for, urgently emphasizes the fundamental need for new rules and global economic coordination.

The economy requires a global governance system, open markets, high economic and social standards, more responsible monetary policy, and stricter supervisory policies, with decision-making bodies and international economic institutions serving as the primary actors at the heart of this new approach.

Europe specifically needs a transparent framework to reduce instabilities exacerbated by globalization, ensure comprehensive reforms aimed at promoting growth, and reduce the debt of individual states.
This is the commitment that this resolution emphasizes for all countries.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I have voted in favor of the report by my esteemed colleague, Mrs. Matera, which examines the situation of single mothers, who today, as already stated, represent more than five percent of the population of the European Union.

The European Union therefore has a moral obligation to protect them, and the text approved today supports policies that can provide concrete assistance to these women, such as measures to support living conditions.
healthcare, flexible work opportunities enabling the balance of private and professional life, priority access to affordable housing, and special unemployment benefits.
Only by adopting these measures can the needs of a category that is increasingly significant in all advanced and industrialized countries be met.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. The budget sets out only a few clear and important objectives within the framework of the EU 2020 strategy. For this reason, thank you to the rapporteurs.
It is essential to focus on investments, and not merely on the prudent maintenance of the budget.
The alternative would be to passively witness the speculative philosophy that characterizes our era.
We can no longer afford to follow individual interests; we must finally represent a single, cohesive subject, ready for confrontation.

I welcome the commitments made regarding pilot projects and preparatory actions under heading two, and I emphasize that funding for the integrated maritime policy must not come at the expense of other initiatives and programs in the fisheries sector.

This will enable us to implement new policies for improved management of marine resources through comprehensive research and data sharing on fish stocks, establishing new planning approaches that in the Adriatic region can serve as best practices for other marine areas.

I conclude with a brief postscript. Within the framework of future debates concerning the CFP, and particularly with regard to the Fisheries Fund, I hope greater effort will be made on fisheries control—the cornerstone of the fight against illegal fishing, and also the foundation for the rebuilding of the United States.
Consider the common carp.
Mr. President, the economic, financial, and social crisis we are currently experiencing has clearly highlighted several weaknesses in the European economic and social model.

I believe one of the most alarming indicators of the current situation is the nearly six million jobs lost over the past three years—figures that are likely to increase further if prompt and effective action is not taken.

The text by colleague Bastos emphasizes that making concrete progress requires greater energy, improved synergy between education, training, and the labor market, combined with policies aimed at ensuring greater alignment between demand and supply in this market.
For these reasons, Mr. President, I have cast my vote in favor.
I voted in favor of what was presented by Member Angelilli, as it addresses an issue that deeply concerns me, both as a mother and as a grandmother.

Protecting children from sexual exploitation, abuse, and the abhorrent world of child pornography means defending our sons and daughters, our grandsons and granddaughters, and thus safeguarding the most vulnerable.
Greater prevention, stricter criminal enforcement against those who commit such offenses, and more careful protection of children on these key points of the document adopted today.
Without doubt, the path is difficult.

I am particularly thinking of the dissemination of child pornography over the internet, which can only be combated through close cooperation among member states, police authorities, and service providers.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the challenges posed by modernity and the rapporteur’s report, to which we have expressed our favorable vote, compel us to place the citizen, in their role as consumer, outside the realm of the market.

center of community debate and remind us that safeguarding its interests cannot remain merely a principle enshrined at the treaty level, but must be a concrete guiding principle for the policies of the European Union.

A well-protected and informed consumer, made more aware and thus able to fully utilize the tools and resources available, and capable of fully enjoying their rights, must be the ultimate goal of the European Union's new strategy for consumer policy.

This strategy must therefore take the form of a broad, all-encompassing approach, extending from the financial services sector to the food industry, from the protection of minors to advertising, and thus be capable of making a significant contribution to Europe's social and environmental objectives.

to counter the possible involvement of organized crime in European sport, calling, as in the text adopted today, for a common definition of sports fraud and the harmonization of sanctions at the European level.
For this reason, I have expressed my favorable vote today.

We welcome this role for the Commission. I say this also as a representative of a country that is currently changing its government— a government that is not technocratic, but a technical government, demonstrating that political forces have not renounced their role.

Then, regarding the theme of stable and fair growth, I believe it must address support for businesses in two points: access to credit and innovation.
The Commission and Europe are able to do so concretely.
At the moment we recapitalize banks, by helping to recapitalize banks—perhaps even with public support—we must insist that this credit reach the real economy, above all the real economy.
At the moment when Horizon 2020 becomes one of the main priorities, we must carry out research largely focused on innovation for enterprises, regardless of their size.
The chapter on the digital agenda, which is also very important because the creation of this single market will be a major assistance for our society.

Policies aimed at combating both new and old poverty, unfortunately on the rise in today's Europe, in the Europe of the recent years' crisis and also of one-sided austerity policies, particularly imposed by states,

Financial commitments, new resources, both on the European budget, within the framework of the financial perspectives, and on European policies, as just recalled by the Social Fund to the ESF, as well as in the budgets of individual Member States.

and especially with regard to individual countries, we should use the European semester to concretely monitor year after year the policies that individual countries dedicate to this fundamental challenge.

To make credible the goal of reducing poverty and social exclusion for around twenty million European citizens, it is therefore necessary to ensure coherence between the substantial resources that Europe must provide, the resources that Member States must make available, and the concrete policies that we can implement.
It is estimated that approximately two billion euros will be needed by 2020 to achieve the objectives we have set ourselves.

This is the challenge we highlight and place at the center of our attention as the Budget Committee: coherence between the financial resources we make available and the policies we put at our disposal.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, there are many workers in difficulty in our countries—elderly people, women, young people, disabled individuals, and millions of people and families brought to their knees by the crisis and speculation.
Faced with this situation, we are here discussing plans to support Roma communities and provide housing for immigrants.
We must think about helping those categories that are truly in difficulty, and provide both financial and political support to the family, which is the institution upon which our society is based.
We must assist small and medium-sized enterprises choked by the crisis, because these SMEs are the true antidote to poverty and the genuine source of wealth in our countries.
We must prioritize our strongest assets, not squander them to provide migrants with a minimum income that Europe intends to impose from above.
Enough talking about Roma and immigrants. Now we must give priority to the truly excluded.
If we look closely around us, we will see many of them, and it is certainly not necessary to enter a Romani camp to see them.
To these excluded groups, Europe must direct its efforts and funding, now more than ever.

Mr. President, I have expressed my favourable vote on the text concerning the work of the ACP Joint Commission, which, thanks to the quality of its work, has established itself as a key player in cooperation between the North and the South of the world.

I myself, as a member of this assembly, have participated in the meetings held in Brussels and in the proceedings conducted in Tenerife in April 2010, and in Kinshasa, in the Congo, in December of the same year.

Precisely thanks to these visits, I was able to witness firsthand, as I had done in 2009 in Angola, the grim conditions endured daily by certain peoples, while simultaneously assessing the efforts made by the ACP to improve matters.

In conclusion, I therefore feel compelled to emphasize, at this particular historical-political juncture—especially for the countries of North Africa and the Middle East—the role played by the CP has been one of...

of fundamental importance, particularly within the framework of overseeing negotiations for economic partnership agreements, by promoting transparency in these processes and supporting the development of disadvantaged local realities.
Mr. President, Honourable Zanicki, personally, I find the establishment of a European heritage label to be interesting.

At a time when, due to the ongoing economic crisis, a certain euroscepticism is emerging in many Member States, I believe that an initiative of this kind could strengthen European citizens' sense of belonging to the Union.

The defining feature of the European brand is not the beauty of a site, but the symbolic value it has represented or continues to represent for the integration of European citizens in their journey toward a more effective intercultural dialogue.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, in December 2009 I participated in the COP Fifteen in Copenhagen as an official member of the European Parliament's delegation.

Approximately two years have passed, and with regret I must say that the work carried out and the commitments undertaken in Copenhagen, subsequently formalized in Cancun, will not be sufficient to achieve the goal of limiting the increase in the planet's average annual temperature to two degrees Celsius.

Some major emitting states, such as Japan or Russia, appear to be distancing themselves from the commitments they have undertaken, while others seem not to assign the appropriate priority to the issue of climate change.

Today, although expressing my favorable vote, I am aware that only through the strong and determined will of heads of state and governments worldwide will we be able to establish the foundations for a comprehensive international agreement.
Internet-related services still hold considerable potential, both for the economy and for a more participatory and inclusive society.
Today, during the transposition phase of the Telecom Package, there is a risk of fragmentation of the European framework in national legislation.
The Commission's monitoring role will therefore be crucial, and I also emphasize BEREC's role, which some of us would have liked to be stronger also in defining the telecom package.

The priorities we wish to emphasize in this debate are network neutrality, the development of broadband infrastructure, and the advancement of more open software, access security, system interoperability, and the promotion of the use of...

digital technologies across various industrial sectors, digital content production and new services, inclusion of those currently excluded—considering people with disabilities, but not only them—defense of informational and cultural pluralism.

It shall furthermore be provided and ensured that operators cannot impose blocks, restrictions, or discrimination against users, and that their transparency in commercial offers and throughout all their activities is maximized.
Mr. President, we are all aware of how important a role the International Criminal Court plays in prosecuting those responsible for war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide.
Unfortunately, in recent times the Court has increasingly become the target of unjustified political attacks.
His arrest warrants have often been ignored, and for this reason, the current budget is not proportional to the efforts and commitment expended.

I therefore express my favourable vote on the text approved today, as it is the European Union's responsibility to undertake all political, diplomatic, and logistical actions to support the Court and ensure its independence and proper, effective functioning.
Thank you, Mr. President. The phosphates present in household laundry detergents end up in water systems, rivers, streams, and lakes across Europe.

Phosphorus triggers an acceleration in the reproduction of various forms of life; consequently, algae and other rapidly developing plant species proliferate excessively when phosphorus levels increase in an aquatic environment.
This excessive algal growth deprives other living organisms of oxygen and can also produce toxins that destroy fish and plant species, thus reducing the water's quality itself.

I therefore expressed my favourable vote on the text adopted today, as it is desirable to reduce the amount of phosphorus discharged into the waters of our continent in order to purify Europe's water resources.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the text presented by colleague Lisek examines the possible measures to be implemented in order to reduce defense costs during a period of financial crisis that is placing a heavy burden on the community budget.
The main proposals concern improved coordination of defense system planning, the pooling and sharing of defense capabilities, and support for technological research and development.
I therefore agree with the rapporteur's position that the economic and financial crisis we have experienced may provide an opportunity for the integration of
the European Union's defense policies, paving the way to develop and finally implement those ambitious reforms that have been stalled for years.
For these reasons, I have cast my vote in favor.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, following the Arab Spring and the events that have ensued, it has become imperative to revise the European Union's neighborhood policy.

It is evident that, given the current situation, relations with the countries concerned must be increasingly focused on cooperation in order to ensure that democracy and security take precedence on both sides of the Mediterranean.

On this matter, I believe that intercultural and interreligious dialogue is essential for strengthening mutual respect and tolerance with our neighboring partner countries, while simultaneously promoting the fight against terrorism and extremism.
For these reasons, Mr. President, I naturally voted in favor.
Thank you, Mr. President. Yesterday in Florence, an event of extreme gravity occurred, causing horror and shock.
A fifty-year-old man opened fire in two markets in the city of Florence, targeting five citizens of Senegalese origin; two of them died, and three are currently hospitalized in critical condition.
The killer then committed suicide.
It is the extreme right-wing racist and xenophobic group that sought to kill people unknown to him, guilty only of having black skin.
Florence is my city, and before being elected to the European Parliament, I served as mayor of Florence for ten years.
Florence is a civic and democratic city, awarded the Gold Medal for its resistance to Nazifascism.
Yet precisely in Florence this abominable racist crime occurred, demonstrating that Nazi-fascist madness can surface anywhere and must never be considered definitively eradicated.

Mr. President, I ask you to express the European Parliament's condolences and to reaffirm this Parliament's commitment against all forms of intolerance and for the respect and rights of every human being.
Mr. President, managing projects in war-affected areas such as Afghanistan cannot be directly compared to the management implemented in countries where the project has been carried out.
in developed countries or countries not affected by conflicts, where aid is evidently hindered even by the stable security situation.
It is necessary, however, to understand the reasons behind the identified gaps—project management and associated risk factors—in order to improve the situation as much as possible.
I share the rapporteur's position in calling on the Commission to conduct a more careful assessment of fraud, corruption, non-compliance, and the effectiveness of specific projects.
It is therefore necessary to increase the transparency and accountability in the use of Union funds and to help both member states and other donors avoid common mistakes.
Mr. President, we do not have the time to go into detail or to engage in high-level debate, but I believe we must acknowledge that there are certain points that remain highly critical.
I share the concern about this green fund at present—where will we find the resources? This remains a major question mark and a significant concern.
However, I believe that to be concrete, we must acknowledge that this time in Durban things have changed.
The old approach has indeed been surpassed, as it no longer reflected the actual global geopolitical situation.
We continue to work on a photograph of the world of the 1990s, an outdated image by now, if viewed in perspective, projected toward the year 2050—truly unsustainable.
We observe that by 2050, nineteen of the world's top thirty economies are expected to be countries that are still currently listed among developing nations.
I believe this shift in approach is perhaps the most politically significant aspect, and it has indeed been accurately highlighted by our colleague Florence.
I also believe that we must no longer proceed in isolated compartments.

Recalling also what Mario Draghi, Governor of the European Central Bank, said just a few days ago, right here in this institution, if we wish to advance, even in this sector, toward those priorities, namely
Growth and employment—we must be truly concrete and focus especially on green economy technologies to genuinely achieve sustainable development and meet the most pressing needs of our citizens.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, Europe is shrinking, stock markets are collapsing, the centralized Europe of financial lobbies is falling apart, yet the Danish Presidency opens its term promising total loyalty to the Commission's plans for creating a super economic government for the continent.
Rather than focusing on the measures needed to save the futures of 500 million citizens, the Danish government pledges to act as a loyal watchdog for eurotechnocracy within the Council.
Other passages, however, I find entirely disagreeable.
First of all, I fail to see how one could today propose as a priority the acceleration of the EU's enlargement process towards the Balkan countries.
There are other issues to address immediately before worrying about how to integrate into Europe countries affected by corruption and crime.
And then Turkey— the Danish government strongly pushes for Turkey's accession.
Fortunately, Turkey is far from meeting the criteria set by the EU for accession.

But the real paradox is that, at a moment when the euro is at risk of failure, it should be the Danish presidency that saves it—the country of Denmark, outside the eurozone, because evidently it has never trusted the euro.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the increase in violations concerning intellectual property has been recorded in recent years...
years risk undermining the intellectual property rights strategy within the single market launched by the Commission in May 2011.
To counteract this phenomenon and safeguard the success of intellectual property protection policy, it has been proposed to entrust certain
tasks related to the management of the European Observatory on Counterfeiting and Piracy established in 2009 at the Office for Harmonization in the Internal Market.

This in order to provide the observatory itself with human and financial resources capable of undertaking actions to combat violations of intellectual property rights and awareness campaigns aimed at increasing consumer awareness of the risks associated with purchasing counterfeit products.

The favorable vote expressed in the report thus marks a significant moment in the fight against the trade in counterfeit goods and in defense of innovation, growth, and competitiveness of small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as of employment and the security of European citizens.
Mr. President, honorable colleagues, intellectual property may appear contradictory in itself as a concept, yet it simultaneously exhibits significant moments of coherence.

I voted in favor of this proposal because its objective—entrusting the European Union Office for the Registration of Marks, Designs and Models with certain responsibilities regarding the management of the European Observatory on Counterfeiting and Piracy—is necessary and indispensable in order to combat violations of intellectual property rights.

The main objectives are to increase awareness among public and private sectors regarding the impact of counterfeit footwear and to inform consumers about the risks associated with purchasing counterfeit products.
Based on the 33 compromise amendments, an agreement was reached on 19 December of this year during the first reading.
The amendments provide that the title of the regulation and the name of the office are not to be changed in the sense of the European Observatory.
Moreover, the important and fundamental aspect is the participation of civil society in the meetings.
All of this will make even more democratic and participatory a moment of synthesis such as the one we are undertaking today through this vote.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the report by Member Lechner fits within that framework of actions aimed at improving services for citizens and businesses throughout the European Union.
The interconnection of central and commercial registers indeed represents an excellent tool for making information more accessible and, above all, faster to obtain for citizens and businesses.
Therefore, Mr. President, I have cast my vote in favor.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, every time the situation appears to improve, new downgrades from rating agencies arrive to shake the course of international markets.
Many European companies are in crisis, and on the employment front, the number of people—especially the young—chronically seeking work is increasing.
If austerity plans and the defense of the euro are necessary measures, even more so are deep reforms of the labor market at the national level, aimed at revitalizing the European economy.
Only by revitalizing competitiveness will the European Union restore confidence among investors and promote the growth of new jobs.
Thank you. Honorable Bizzotto. I do not deny that the rights of homosexuals must be protected. What I deny is that, as is the case in Italy, homosexual families do not exist.

Homosexuals will never exist, simply because the family that must be protected— the family that even the European Parliament should protect—is the traditional family, composed of a woman, a man, and their own children.
That's all, it seems to me a rather elementary matter.
This is the reality in Italy and the other twenty-three countries.

Mr. President, first of all, I too would like to join my colleague Lechner in extending a warm greeting, and likewise share in the assessment of his work, which has proceeded with competence, tenacity, and determination.

A painstaking task; occasionally, this draft regulation would get stuck, and he would bring it out again, restarting negotiations with the Council, with the Commission, with the legal committee—I believe he can be proud of this.
I see that there is unanimous agreement regarding him.

Turning to our present regulation, I am also convinced that this is an important matter. Perhaps it does not make headlines in the newspapers, perhaps it is not regarded as high politics, yet the construction of the European Union also passes through...
through work that fulfills citizens' interests in their daily lives, and especially those millions and millions who live straddling different states, and thus are part of us
they encounter practices that are difficult to untangle, tedious, and sometimes touch on sensitivities at the heart of a family that politics generally finds hard to address.
The European Union should be proud to have such an approach at its foundation.

The solutions that have been proposed are intelligent and innovative. For example, individuals making a will have been granted the option of choosing between their habitual residence or their country of origin.
and this makes a decision more flexible, given the very deep, truly profound differences between one state and another, which could constantly complicate the issue of succession.
However, legal certainty has also been established, because once the legal solution—either habitual residence or country of origin—has been chosen, it cannot

A limitation of judicial discretion is established, as well as a limitation of conflicts that may arise, thus providing a more peaceful approach in this area; therefore, there is greater freedom and greater certainty.

And then the certificate of succession—we believe that certificates are of little importance, but this creates the conditions under which the heir enjoys greater agility and certainty when facing any possible difficulties that may arise.
It is a fact that positively characterizes this law.

And then it's true, this law is not perfect; there are issues concerning the executor, the feasibility of direct inheritance, and so on. However, I don't wish to utter one more politically charged remark.
We hope the Council will approve this text, we hope it becomes a regulation, but we must not place a tombstone on this text, because we cannot even entertain the idea of doing so.
England in a different position from Great Britain, thus, because Europe cannot do without Great Britain and therefore we should proceed to overcome these difficulties subsequently.
Thank you, sir.
Mr. President, the European Union has produced an enormous amount of legislation and regulations to build European law, and has created the European area known as the Stockholm Programme.
There is a discrepancy between this body of legislation, this legislative monument, and the extent to which citizens actually use it, which is numerically and proportionally very low.

On the other hand, national judges and national lawyers of the individual Member States have grown up within the legal system of their respective countries and have predominantly received training in this context.
meaning, whereas we believe the true European judge is the national judge who transforms their mindset and becomes imbued with knowledge of European law.

For this reason, before discussing training, it is essential to engage in extensive communication with citizens and legal professionals, so that they become advocates of this major innovation and help foster the willingness to take advantage of the benefits offered by this right.

But above all, it must be a bottom-up movement that develops within in-service training. One cannot conduct lessons as if addressing schoolchildren in judicial training for adults who are highly professional.

One must stimulate curiosity when handling one's own cases, connect with other colleagues, and seek solutions, as today's common law and civil law systems are converging and are no longer as distant from each other as they once were within the legal culture.

This is the crucial point. In our proposals—and I am pleased that the European Commission has come to recognize these aspects—we are calling for a reversal of trend, from the bottom up, with more legal studies and less judicial training.
There is an example in the Netherlands called Eurinfra, which is very interesting: it is a network of officials in each court who disseminate this knowledge and encourage the judges in this regard.
The essence of our project is this, and we hope it will receive the Commission's full support.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, I would first like to express my sincere appreciation for the work carried out by my colleague Giegold on this own-initiative report, and I also wish to recall that together with him, and with others, we actively participate in the Parliament's Social Economy Intergroup.

My political group, as you know, considers the social model one of the foundational elements of the project of European integration, not merely one policy among many, but a defining feature of our very project.

And in these times, with attacks also linked to the need for public spending cuts, etc., we still convincingly defend this priority, knowing that it can be an updated, modified social model, but we also believe it must be maintained, defended, and reaffirmed precisely as a priority.

Therefore, in this same vision, we must also recognize and strengthen economic subjects engaged in enterprise and the production of goods and services, such as cooperatives, which play a very specific role in the so-called European social economy.

They emphasize that production can be carried out, good results can be achieved, and efficiency can be maintained even without profit as the primary objective, provided that other goals are pursued, such as employment, welfare, the valorization of work, and contribution to the life of the local community.

That is why, Mr. President, I conclude, it is essential that Europe provide the necessary tools for an active policy and establish a sound statute—not the current unusable one, but a good statute for cooperatives could be a way to support this vital sector.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the role of the cooperative movement has always been crucial in building a more balanced and fair economic dimension.
And at this moment, as we are experiencing one of the most severe economic and social crises since the war, the role of cooperative societies becomes crucial.
There has been a strong development of the cooperative dimension in the economy, resulting in increased employment, more skilled work, and a more balanced redistribution of produced wealth.
It is therefore positive that the European Union has adopted a statute for cooperative societies, but now more than ever, genuine simplification of the rules is necessary.
We need to give new momentum to the creation of a common European framework in the field of cooperation, and this must be achieved by providing support rather than creating bureaucratic obstacles.
For this reason, I voted in favor of this resolution and I hope the Commission and the Council will act actively to address the issues mentioned.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the salvation of millions of unemployed Europeans does not depend on the subtle assistance provided by the European Globalisation Adjustment Fund. The data collected reveal that the majority of Fund beneficiaries fail to secure new employment, while the more fortunate are reintegrated into the labor market only through fixed-term contracts, sometimes as short as six months.
In many cases, the unemployed choose to forgo this fund because they doubt its usefulness.
Haymaking is not a solution, nor does it mask the disastrous consequences of a European trade policy dangerously and too lightly managed by the Commission.

To combat the scourge of unemployment, now structural in nature, Europe must curb its excessive openness to the markets of developing countries, whose unfair competition is the primary cause of our companies' failures.
As long as Europe continues to promote economic self-harm by maintaining a short-sighted stance toward global trade flows, our companies will keep failing.
The fund can only provide temporary support, but it is not through training courses that our unemployed will find peace and security.
I believe that today represents a significant achievement for the European Parliament.
President Buzek, when stating that we must establish an anti-mafia commission, I recall that many colleagues from various countries claimed that the mafia is solely a problem of Sicily and Italy.

Today, having recognized by colleagues from across northern Europe that a problem of organized crime exists in Europe, and having the will to fight it together, I believe this is a significant and important development. I would like to say thank you, Mr. President, and thank you to the entire Parliament.
Thanks to Mr. Sondal for such a comprehensive presentation.

I would also like, above all, to reaffirm our solidarity with the families of the victims of the serious clashes that occurred on December 16 in Zanusdaen, as well as our concern regarding the subsequent development of events.

What began as essentially a labor warning degenerated into the serious incidents in which 17 people lost their lives, and in the days following the incidents, into the arrests of journalists and opposition representatives.

It is in everyone's interest, and as Kazakhstan has also demonstrated firsthand, to shed full light on the events. I hope that today's session will stand out uniquely as a positive opportunity for our Parliament to further examine what has occurred so far.

and reiterate our appeal for an investigation that will reassure the international community that responsibilities regarding the events in Zanonzhen will be clarified and those responsible will be prosecuted, in full respect for the principles of legality and the protection of human rights.

The delegation which I have the honor of leading has had the opportunity, during several of its meetings, to hear the explanations provided by Kazakh authorities as well as those from several very active NGOs monitoring events in this country.

The willingness of the Kazakh authorities to provide explanations, as well as their promises of further clarifications, are undoubtedly positive elements that must naturally be appreciated by all of us.
For this reason as well, I would like to emphasize the need to maintain a certain balance—balance that I had hoped would be clearly evident in the text of the resolution we will vote on tomorrow.

It might have been advisable to postpone the decision pending the conclusion of the official investigations, which would have benefited from the accompaniment of an official mission by international bodies.

The undeniable need to achieve greater clarity regarding what occurred, and the necessity to appeal for increasingly greater efforts by Kazakhstan to advance democratic progress, must also be consistently accompanied by a renewed commitment and an open, constructive dialogue with this country.

Mr. President, I voted in favor of this proposal because it aims to update the regulations from 2004 and 2009 in order to align with the changes in the national legislation of Member States concerning social security and to keep pace with social developments affecting the coordination of these systems.

This proposal also incorporates recommendations from the Administrative Commission for the Coordination of Security Systems, whose purpose is to improve, modernize, and acquire, in accordance with the provisions of Article 72, paragraph F, of the previous regulation.

The changes contained in this proposal are intended, indeed, to ensure legal certainty for the parties concerned, and furthermore aim to ensure appropriate coordination at the level of the European Union of national social security systems.

It also reflects the regulatory changes implemented at the national level and was necessary to adopt in order to take into account recent developments regarding cross-border mobility that affect the coordination of social security systems.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the credibility of the European Union on the international stage and the effectiveness of its policies on human rights undoubtedly depend on condemning violations, abuses, and breaches of human rights.

committed within or outside armed conflicts, violations against women, minors, and in general the most vulnerable, and persecution based on religion or belief, but it is equally essential to avoid disparities

of criteria between the European Union's human rights policy and its other external policies, thus promoting democratization and supporting the fight against social inequalities, particularly during economic crises such as the current one.
Mr. President, I thank my colleagues Asenís, Patraoneves, Romeva, and their assistants for their substantial contribution to the success of this initiative.

Fish is a common good, as it provides fifteen percent of the proteins consumed by more than three billion people, and the livelihoods of over five hundred million people depend on fishing and aquaculture.
This makes it a true global public good.
The approval of this statement indicates to the Commission a clear path regarding the necessity of promoting responsible conduct, both among fishing communities and in consumer behavior.

The debate on the reform of the common fisheries policy now has an additional reason to support actions that move European policy beyond the narrow focus on methods of resource extraction and a regulatory framework solely oriented inward.

On the contrary, a strategy focused on the sea is recommended as a preferred setting to enhance its reproductive capacity, both in inland waters and through innovative fishing agreements with third countries.

Moreover, all this is not only environmental protection and the preservation of biodiversity, but also responsible fishing, economic and social sustainability, and the clear interest of fishermen and our economies.
Thank you, Mr. Chair.
1.083.2006, submitted by the European Commission shortly thereafter, contains provisions aimed at establishing a European instrument for risk sharing.

The objective is indeed to address the difficulties encountered by certain Member States in securing the private financing required to implement investment projects that can be funded only partially through public funds.

Although the path toward exiting the economic crisis remains long, I consider the work accomplished to reach a compromise text fruitful, as it has established a cap for funds allocated to the financial instrument and better defined the risk-sharing mechanisms.
Please, esteemed colleagues, the economic crisis, technological development, and demographic changes have transformed European society and the labor market in recent years.
To meet future skill demands, many people will need to complete higher education, and many workers will need to continuously acquire and update their skills and knowledge while already employed.

For this reason, I have voted in favor of the colleague Tocque's text, which aims at modernizing higher education systems in order to achieve the objectives set out in the Europe 2020 strategy and to promote social, cultural, and economic development within the Union.
Yes, thank you, Mr. President. I won’t take up much time, as I would like to merely touch briefly on a few points.

I believe this seventh environmental action programme can undoubtedly prove to be a useful tool, provided it is based on a vision of the future that takes into account the need to maintain a robust European industrial base, which is essential for sustaining a decent and acceptable level of employment.
There are some things that worry me.
An absolute priority is that this program ensures the most effective implementation of existing regulations before establishing

additional binding objectives, and I am deeply convinced that in order to address today's environmental challenges, we must rely on future environmental policies grounded in solid evidence and the most up-to-date scientific knowledge.

I am concerned about Article 23, which presents ecological footprint as an absolute indicator; it is, in fact, an indicator, an empirical model of reality, not reality itself, and we must keep this clearly in mind.

This has many limitations; it tells us how much we impact the environment, but it absolutely does not assess, for example, the quality of the environment. Therefore, my concerns remain, and I urge everyone to take them into account.
When we speak of good examples for citizens, when we speak of the internal market and of the possibilities to create opportunities for growth,
authentic and concrete opportunities for change in everyday life through the internal market—we can truly draw on this example, though clearly we will need many more, as a result of our work.

reduced, but because they can increase the supply of services and, in essence, also help the economy as a whole. Therefore, I believe this is undoubtedly a positive example, a good example.

However, you will have understood, Mr. Commissioner, that there is a very widespread will in this Parliament, particularly I refer to the interventions by two of my colleagues, Marturel Goebbels, from my group, as well as many others.
others who would like this good example to become excellent, and who envision us moving—partly anticipated by this measure—toward a zero roaming outcome, because it is possible.

Every European citizen must be able to travel freely between Member States, enjoying the telephone tariff set by their national operator.

End frauds and exorbitant bills for those traveling within the EU. If we truly wish to provide a concrete response to citizens, we must therefore establish a Schengen area for telecommunications, eliminating different tariffs for citizens depending on the country they are in.
Mr. President, colleagues, I was part of the European Parliament delegation that visited Sicily last November.
Our head of delegation, Cecilia Wistrom, wrote a letter to the Italian minister; the Interior Minister, Chancellor, responded, providing us with fairly clear answers.

I wish to emphasize above all the fact that following that visit to Italy, the government changed, the approach to the situation changed as well, and the situation itself evolved in the countries from which the migratory flow predominantly originated.
This, however, must not prevent us from a careful analysis of what has occurred and of the current situation.
The most significant issue that remains relevant today is Lampedusa's status as an unsafe port, and the closure of the first-aid and reception center at Contrade Embriagola following the fire
September 2011, which damaged it only partially—namely, one dormitory destroyed, one damaged out of seven buildings in the complex—and then the de facto cancellation of the project

Now, aside from the fact that in the Mediterranean the only unsafe port was Tripoli during Gaddafi's time, and this comparison does not seem particularly flattering to me, given that migration flows, although significantly reduced in number—even for seasonal reasons—

They have certainly not ceased. It is crucial that a first aid and reception service operates effectively in Lampedusa, which is the first point of arrival for those coming from the other shore after journeys that are by no means easy or risk-free. The Somali shipwreck survivors know this well.

in April of this year, after witnessing ten people die on the boat they were traveling in, or the twenty who were transferred from Linosa because there was no space in other reception centers, or the eighteen who still live in the stadium changing rooms on Linosa.
