Everything depends on the goodwill and kindness of the on-duty carabinieri or of the citizens and volunteers of Lampedusa.
Another consideration to make is the role that the center itself had assumed in the recent past.

Allowing thousands upon thousands of people to accumulate in a place that, due to its capacity and intended function, could only accommodate a few hundred, in clear violation of every legal and ethical standard, is...

Allowing a territory of just a few square kilometers—already challenging due to its location and logistical difficulties—to be forced to accommodate a number of people exceeding its own population must be acknowledged.
reflect on the responsibilities for a situation of extreme tension that subsequently led to
the fire itself.
I believe the excellent report by colleague Rinaldi has been successful in the Chamber.
I fully share the highly favorable assessment of the report.
Nevertheless, I would like to raise a small question: our Europe, in relation to this new world striving to find democracy and different economic conditions, is a Europe divided by the Mediterranean.

It is precisely a particular attention to the Mediterranean that I would like the Commission to focus on, both in terms of environmental aspects and in terms of the economic transactions taking place in the Mediterranean.
The Mediterranean is the fishing area.
We are establishing a new fishing policy in Europe that will set very strict rules from Europe's side.
On the other hand, however, there are no rules.
And so why not use fishing agreements and provide support to these countries so that they implement the same rules applied in Europe?

This would be beneficial for European fishing, beneficial for the sea, for environmental conservation, and for ensuring that this rift becomes increasingly less divided, and instead increasingly a unified space upon which to build Europe's future and that of this new world.

Following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the European Parliament has become a full-fledged institutional actor in the field of security policies and therefore has the right to actively and decisively participate in shaping the priorities of the internal security strategy and the Union's model of security.

The rapporteur Borsellino's report identifies the five key areas for which concrete actions have been proposed and calls for greater judicial and police cooperation among Member States to combat organized crime and terrorism.

I consider it particularly important to give strong consideration to the development of prevention policies, which are a fundamental tool for ensuring a space of freedom, security, and justice in Europe.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, with the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the foundations have been laid for the development of an integrated criminal law within Community law.

Keeping in mind the importance of mutual recognition and the harmonization of protective standards, it is essential to emphasize that the law must be extremely clear and readily understandable, so that it is evident to everyone which actions constitute or do not constitute a crime.
This particularly applies to directives that must be mutually compatible with national law.
Any lack of clarity, in fact, could lead to divergent interpretations of the law.

I therefore believe that only a close collaboration among European institutions regarding the principles and methods to be followed can ensure a coherent and high-quality European strategy in the field of criminal law.
Mr. President, the European Union safeguards the general interests of consumers against unfair business practices, misleading advertising, and abusive contractual terms.
There is no doubt that consumers who are more vulnerable due to age, physical or mental health issues, or simply a lack of adequate information must be protected more carefully.
I therefore express my favourable vote on the text drafted by my colleague Perez, as I agree that the creation of a strategy for consumer law
the so-called more vulnerable groups not only contributes to their social inclusion but also helps ensure a safer, fairer, and more competitive single market.
Mr. President, the report by our colleague Perez, which we have expressed our favorable vote on, addresses the need to consider the development of a
a strategy aimed at protecting the rights of the most vulnerable consumers, in view of the strategy that the European Commission and the Member States will be called upon to develop in this regard.
The conditions of consumer vulnerability arise from causes of different natures and may potentially affect everyone.

For this reason, it is necessary to provide protection for this category alongside the general protection of consumers and to develop a targeted strategy in this regard, which, by taking into account the composite nature of the issue, avoids

on one hand, the formulation of static and rigid definitions incapable of accounting for all the nuances of the issue and the various possible forms of vulnerability, and on the other, preventing the creation of new forms of discrimination.
It is therefore necessary to establish specific and targeted protection measures.

Thank you, Mr. President. The management of bluefin tuna fishing has a long and rather complex history dating back to the 1990s and is today probably the most strictly regulated by the European Union.
Despite significant investments in monitoring and enforcement having led to greater compliance with regulations, uncertainties remain regarding the issue of overfishing.
I therefore expressed my support for the Romeva i Rueda report, which proposes a reduction in the allowable catch rate, enhanced monitoring, and strengthened measures to reduce fishing capacity.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the relations between the European Union and China clearly reveal situations of imbalance at the commercial level.
It is therefore incumbent upon European institutions to strive to assist European businesses in overcoming the crisis and to make access to international markets more equitable.
I have therefore expressed my support for this report, particularly sharing the rapporteur's concerns regarding the application of the principle of reciprocity to
relations between the European Union and China to ensure fair competition in international markets and the demand for greater protection of intellectual property rights.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the road ahead is still long, but today can be an important step toward the eventual introduction and implementation of a tax on financial transactions.
I understand the prudence, caution, and need for gradualism expressed by Commissioner Scemeta, as well as the technical issues rightly highlighted by Minister Vestager.

But precisely for this reason, I hope the Parliament will express a clear political will, by a broad majority, to provide momentum and impetus to the necessary initiatives aimed at addressing the issues that remain unresolved, particularly at the level of the Council.
We all want to carefully assess the impact of this tax, but it is unfair to stir up unfounded fears, which may lead to contradictory and counterproductive decisions.
I am referring, for example, to the logic underlying the proposal of exemptions.
If one is attentive to minimizing the tax's impact, then it is important to have the broadest possible tax base with a low rate, rather than seeking to extend exemptions indefinitely.
To those concerned about the impact on small investors, I remind them of the progressive nature of the tax, which affects more heavily those who carry out a greater number of transactions.
transactions, and it is clear that the competent authorities will have to monitor, in any case, that efforts are not made to shift any potential costs onto customers and end users.
I therefore hope that the Parliament will deliver a vote by a large majority in favor of this tax.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, since 1990, through the instrument of macro-financial assistance, the European Union has provided economic aid to countries facing short-term balance of payments difficulties in the form of grants, loans, or a combination of both.
The rather lengthy timelines, added to the difficulties highlighted by the decision-making criteria used so far, have made it necessary to formulate some amendments to the regulation.

I therefore welcome the proposal introduced in the new regulation, which aims to make evaluation criteria faster and more effective, and to establish clearer rules and conditions for the disbursement of macro-financial assistance.
Mr. President, honourable colleagues, there are approximately one hundred million European citizens engaged in various forms of voluntary work.

From major sporting events to cultural gatherings, volunteers play a crucial role, and one must not overlook the work of those who rush to provide assistance and solidarity in the event of severe natural or environmental disasters.
Volunteering represents one of the broadest expressions of active citizenship and a crucial activity for the development of democratic values and equality within the European Union.
For this reason, I advocate the development of high-quality volunteerism, both national and cross-border, and the elimination of existing barriers.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, from communications to finance, transportation, energy, culture, and health, the role played by technology is becoming increasingly significant.
The increasingly frequent use of the Internet and digital tools makes the issue of cybersecurity one of the priorities on the European agenda.
Information and communication technologies can indeed foster economic and social development only if existing regulations ensure user security regarding data privacy and intellectual property rights.

For this reason, only a comprehensive international cooperation involving member states, institutions, and industry stakeholders can establish a robust system capable of implementing effective measures to ensure cybersecurity.
Madam President, thanks to the Commissioner, she has said important and fully shareable things.
I find it truly, as he said, unsettling that we have each lived in our respective countries with the figures and data we previously heard from colleague Oconi.
Yet it is equally clear that this scandal has truly highlighted the need for a careful assessment of the current European legislation on medical devices.

Unfortunately, this fraud has highlighted the shortcomings we have discussed so far in court, which the commissioner has so effectively outlined; therefore, I would prefer not to dwell on this aspect within the limited time available.
I believe it is essential to mobilize everything necessary so that we can offer the best possible to our citizens.
But I believe this scandal, which has taught us so much, could also serve as a starting point to take an additional step forward, and I echo what my colleague just said.

The communication directed toward patients is truly inadequate; only the positive aspects are ever emphasized, with claims of a kind of eternal youth and promised beauty, yet no prostheses are absolutely risk-free.

I believe this awareness, especially when discussing purely aesthetic surgery, must be communicated to people, to young women, because we know there are very young individuals who are approaching this type of procedure.
I see that you are convinced and therefore I urge you: let's really get to work, the time is short, we should have already done it yesterday.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, the Council's regulation concerning the multiannual plan for ring net stock was adopted prior to the entry into force of the Treaty and therefore alignment has become necessary.

This regulation, the first in the fisheries sector to be aligned with the Treaty, therefore holds considerable importance, as the solution adopted in this case could affect the decision-making procedure applicable to other multiannual plans.
For this reason, I cast my vote in favor.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, from Seveso to Bhopal to Toulouse, there have been numerous environmental disasters in recent years that have affected Europe and put the health of European citizens at risk.

The text by colleague Ader concerns the revision of the Services Directive or two, which aims to limit the effects of those incidents involving significant quantities of hazardous and highly polluting substances dispersed into the environment.

The revised regulations, which will come into force starting in June 2011, aim to protect the environment and, above all, to prevent tragic health consequences for citizens; therefore, I have voted in favor.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I too thank Stevenson for the excellent work and the approach he has given to this report, which substantially addresses the two main issues.

Here it is not merely a matter of a herring management plan; fundamentally, it is about securing the future for fishermen in certain respects and ensuring marine planning so that, over the long term, the sea is properly protected.
But the key issue of this report remains the matter of delegated acts.

I believe the Council should accept the approach set out in the Treaty of Lisbon, and I also think it is now time for the Parliament, in some way, with a voice perhaps more authoritative than that of the European Parliament,
debate within the Fisheries Committee or a debate on a report concerning trawling, dare the courage to take a very clear and firm stance on the issue of delegated acts.
We assert the right that an ordinary procedure truly applies to these matters.
So perfectly that the issue also involves, in addition to many other fishing sectors—some of which will worsen compared to the ongoing reform—matters related to agriculture and other topics.
The essential step is respecting the will of Parliament.
In preparing this report, no account whatever was taken of the interested parties themselves, the transport companies.
In Italy, 91 percent of freight transport takes place by road.
Their rights will be ignored if this report is approved in its current form.
In many committees of this Parliament, we daily strive to reduce the financial and administrative burden weighing on our businesses, but reports like this undermine those efforts.
all our efforts, as they will suffocate the companies themselves with new bureaucratic burdens, ruinous economic losses, and new forms of unfair competition.

It would have taken very little to achieve a balanced report that did not overlook the right to survival of our companies during a period of great economic difficulty, but such a dialogue was not possible.

On the contrary, the report has deliberately ignored even previous legislative acts of the European Union, such as the 2002 Working Time Directive, and has even overlooked European Commission documents acknowledging the dangers associated with practices such as retrofitting, which are instead reintroduced in this report.
The document we will vote on tomorrow, colleagues, will ask you to forget the positive achievements made in recent years, and once again, it will be European companies that will bear the costs.

Just to say, Mr. President, that ACTA was a very serious political mistake—setting aside the legal aspect—it triggered a reaction worldwide of immense proportions, and that alone is sufficient to prove it was a political error.
Why the benzene?
because it created the suspicion that actual access to the web was being closed off, which would be a major modern revolution.
And why is that?
because he made the mistake of combining counterfeiting and piracy, which are two clearly distinct phenomena.
The way of fighting counterfeiting has nothing to do with copyright.
And then yet another error that excluded Parliament from an important activity it has been carrying out for some time, namely the creation of a new copyright, a new right of authorship.

the old copyright law that existed before the web. Then, the point is that we must work on copyright law and set ACTA aside, as it is an obstacle, and reject and exclude it, because it hinders our work.
Fortunately, after months and years of hesitation, the Commission has brought two significant measures—on orphan works and the upcoming one on copyright management societies—before the Parliament.
This is the advantage. We work on this by reflecting with our minds, not merely with emotion, yet without being hindered by bureaucracy.
Mr. President, I simply wish to add my voice to the acclaim this report has received in the House regarding the need to strengthen the capacity of the European Union
of being a place where it is possible to invest, produce, and produce with the aim of our vision of an inclusive economy, socially and environmentally sustainable.

Therefore, I believe some of these guidelines here are very useful within this measure, others can be added, and I think it is important to gather this indication, working increasingly toward enhancing attractiveness.
consequence of territorial policies, of territorial marketing, of openness whereby the territory itself proposes a productive, industrial vocation.

manufacturing and service enterprises that, emerging from the territory, respect all environmental and simultaneously European policy compatibilities moving in this direction, generating from the territory and from Europe a voice that attracts investment and fosters growth.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, when discussing sensitive issues such as the authorization of products and substances for use in organic production, we are talking about safeguarding the health of European citizens.

It is indeed important to meet consumer expectations regarding the quality of organic products circulating in European markets, and it is equally important to ensure the proper application of Community legislation by relevant authorities, bodies, and operators.
For this reason, I express my favourable vote on the colleague's text.

Mr. President, honourable colleagues, following the initial comments on the Council, we have now entered the phase of reductionist reading, which is incorrect because significant progress has been made.
Consider, for example, the breaking of destructive ties between banks and public debts, and the admittedly weak plan for employment and work.

It seems the spirit of revitalization and renewed commitment to political unity is once again emerging within European institutions, after so much sluggishness and inertia, and a genuine will to achieve tangible progress toward deeper integration.
Yesterday we recorded a strong alignment between Parliament and the Commission on this issue.

I am not speaking of winners and losers, but it is undeniable that there has been a new institutional and political vitality compared to the previous diarchy, which was the negation, indeed the very antithesis, of the communal spirit.
There has been an initiative and activism by multiple countries not to create new exclusive alliances, but to build a common consensus respectful of all member states.
This is the path toward the political objective of unity, without which any banking, fiscal, and monetary union will be entirely inadequate.
On June 20, with the Council's decision to restore to Member States the possibility of reinstating border controls, a new Europe was born—a Europe brave enough to fulfill the desire of...
greater security for its citizens, finally inspired by a more mature interpretation of the concept of solidarity, brought back to the defense of rights, above all those of its peoples.

To President Schulz, even though you are not here, I say that this historic decision does not represent an interinstitutional incident, but rather a respect for the will of the citizens—and not for that euro-bureaucracy which for years has pretended not to understand what is truly happening beyond the glass palace.
No imaginary influx of irregular migrants, Mr. President Schulz, but an increase in illegal immigrants, measured by Frontex, of thirty-five percent in 2011.

I regret only that the scenes of political sterilization enacted in this Chamber during the previous plenary session deprived us of time that could have been devoted to a more useful and constructive discussion on why it was not us, but the Member States, who defended the interests of our citizens.

If the Parliament fails to find its own soul and the courage to go against complacency and sterile clichés on which it has been drifting for too long, it will continue to be a follower rather than a leader in building the European project.
Mr. President, I would like to draw the attention of this House to the fact that yesterday...
officially announced that the Higgs boson has been measured and concretely identified.
the particle that gives mass to all others and their existence, and this occurred in European soil.
Science gives life to our land, civilization, and well-being, and for a moment we may aspire to once again become a world capital of science.
Utopia is the trait of the human being who invents a utopia and desires to realize it, and it is for science and for this wonderful ambition of humanity that today a person
A blind person who has no eyes can see with their hands or read a book with their ears, and a young South African boy who has no legs can run in the Olympics in a sprint competition.
Well then, Europe must become the homeland of science. This major event, which will open up new frontiers of knowledge, must be fully supported by Europe.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, victims of crime within the European Union too often refrain from filing complaints due to fear or lack of trust in institutions.
The text adopted today aims to strengthen the rights of crime victims and ensure them the same level of protection, assistance, and access to justice across all countries.
all Member States, paying particular attention to those victims who are at high risk of suffering further harm or intimidation during the criminal proceedings.

Of particular importance, therefore, is the recognition of victim status not only for individuals who have suffered harm as a result of a crime, but also for family members and the deceased person following the crime.
On the eleventh of September, the Catalan people peacefully demonstrated in Barcelona.
One million people have requested independence for Catalonia from Spain.
On October 15 of last year, the British Prime Minister Cameron signed the agreement to allow a referendum on Scotland's independence from London.
In Veneto, my region, in recent weeks thousands of people have signed in order to hold a referendum on Veneto's independence from Italy.
Yesterday, in Veneto, Lombardy, and all the regions of northern Italy, hundreds of thousands of people signed petitions demanding greater autonomy from Rome.
Yet even in Flanders and the Basque Country, citizens are loudly demanding autonomy and independence for national states.
What does President Schulz say?
It calls for a courageous commitment to clearly say no to all separatist tendencies.
His deputy, Vidal Cuadras, went even further, openly calling for the use of force against Catalan citizens.
Their statements constitute a most serious act, contrary to the principles of the democracy that we here proclaim and that we should defend.
I tell them that it will be the peoples who will write the future of the new Europe, not the bureaucrats in Brussels.
The path is already marked.
The strict economic policy adopted to overcome the crisis has not yielded the expected results.
As highlighted in the report by colleague Lavia, only by investing in a more selective manner and supporting the ideal of a European budget aimed at
Supporting growth and employment will make it possible to rebalance the economies of the Member States, thereby improving the situation of European citizens.
For this reason, I therefore express my vote in favor.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the social and economic context in which the multiannual financial framework for the period 2014–2020 is being discussed is rather problematic.

With member states committed to implementing fiscal adjustments within their respective national budgets to address public finances, the European Union is viewed in some cases as an entity that exacerbates the financial burden on taxpayers.

For this reason, to truly overcome the crisis and secure a better future for young Europeans, it will be of fundamental importance to support increased and more concrete investments in the fields of training and development.
This is the programmatic charter of an institution. From the budget one can understand what it is, what it does, and above all what it intends to achieve in the future.

We face a crucial choice between a survival budget, a mere continuation of the status quo, perhaps slightly less burdensome for everyone, or a budget of courage, of selective choices, of investments in employment, innovation, and young people—that is, in the future of the European Union and its vocation for development.

Everyone should understand that, as colleague Kalfin clearly stated among others, Europe today can achieve what individual states can no longer accomplish for the economy, regional development, and territories, where social cohesion either establishes or undermines the lives of the citizens we have discussed.

Today, Europe's major decisions risk being stifled by accounting disputes within the Council, not only among beneficiary and contributor countries, but also between net recipient and net contributing nations.

We argue that this is a false problem, because it is easy to demonstrate, using figures and numbers—not words—what has occurred in the past, that contributing countries, sometimes more than others, benefit from economic convergence and growth.
The Council is currently working to assign figures to the various headings.

The war of positions has begun: statements and positions taken by certain states that regard the budget as just any other expenditure that can be reduced without remorse, indeed, one that may even serve to fuel an easily exploited anti-European sentiment during times of economic crisis.
It is a mistaken position that does not aid the integration we are striving to achieve in other areas.
Dear colleagues, our Europe remains incomplete and mutilated, as we have often stated.
We adopted a stability pact that was supposed to also promote development, but we have stabilized so much that we are now in recession.
Here, let us at least seize the opportunity provided by this budget, this multiannual framework, to make choices in the right direction.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the lack of information and awareness regarding one's own rights, compounded by obstacles,
Linguistic barriers and the non-recognition of diplomas and qualifications are the most common problems encountered by EU citizens when residing, studying, or working in another EU country.
Unfortunately, there remains a lack of visibility regarding the concept of Union citizenship and its concrete benefits.
For this reason, I have expressed my support for 2013 as the European Year of Citizens.

The primary objective is therefore to strengthen awareness of the rights associated with European citizenship and to assist citizens in fully exercising their right to move freely and reside freely within the territories of the Member States.
Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the growing number of innovative financial instruments presents numerous challenges in terms of regulation, governance, and monitoring their effectiveness.
A proper balance must indeed be found between the objectives of transparency and oversight, as well as a sufficient level of effectiveness and speed of implementation.

The introduction of such instruments at the European level is nonetheless of great importance, as it represents a means through which the European Union, particularly during times of crisis, can stimulate investment in the economy.
in a manner consistent with their own objectives, in a context where the ongoing decline in contributions destined for the EU budget contrasts with the steady increase in its political ambitions.
Brussels I Regulation has undoubtedly represented one of the most effective instruments
of Union legislation, as it has laid the foundations for a European judicial area and promoted legal certainty among European citizens.
The amendment of this regulation touches upon fundamental issues for the development of European justice, including the extension of rules on
on the jurisdiction over disputes involving parties domiciled in third countries and the agreements concerning the choice of competent forum.
I am convinced that the adoption of the new regulation can ensure stronger consumer protection and greater legal certainty within the internal market, and for this reason I have voted in favor.

Mr. Commissioner, today you have called upon Europe to assume a new role in coordinating industrial policies, and even the most stringent advocates of autonomy and private initiative now demand the exercise of this role.
Without a greater European integration of national strategies, even in terms of employment protection, the current crisis will not be overcome and there will be no revival.
The crisis in the automobile industry is not only one of overproduction capacity; it is a dramatic loss of employment.
But the crisis now appears structural and concerns the very fate of the automobile and the product as it exists today.
We want to keep the automobile industry in Europe and we want to avoid dispersing existing expertise, know-how, and professional skills.
We therefore call for the initiation of a major restructuring and revitalization project, and we urge that this be done promptly, as costs are proportional to time.
Restructuring cannot be merely the result of fragmented national decisions or individual firms.
At the very least, we must attempt to coordinate and steer this painful process at the community level, bringing the relevant parties together around the table through social dialogue.
producers, trade unions, and national governments, offering the support of research, innovation, human capital training, requalification, and the necessary funding.
I know it will be a difficult task because the national interests of individual companies are not aligned.

But since the current competitive struggle within the European Union is highly ambiguous and sometimes risks being unfair, I believe it is ultimately in the interest of all countries to have effective community-driven impetus and leadership to jointly confront the increasingly aggressive global market.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, the energy crisis has already imposed, and continues to impose, an extremely high cost on employment, affecting thousands of workers—both precarious and stable, as well as temporary workers—who are numerous in this sector.
The major steel groups are increasingly shifting toward the Far East or the American continent, raising a significant question about the direction of our development model.
I believe Europe must regain the understanding that a manufacturing system such as ours requires robust basic industries capable of supplying the European internal market.
We must therefore defend this sector and the role of a stable steel industry; we will support it, Mr. Commissioner, if we wish to halt industrial decline.

I believe we must move beyond a dualism that has not been reached here this evening, yet is often heard in discussions on this subject—the dualism between environmental advocates and workers' interests. This is incorrect, as it prevents us from seeing a viable vision for the future.
An industry that regards steelmaking as an integral component requires advanced production systems and concrete, balanced solutions that safeguard diverse interests and different needs.

Therefore, we need strong, even European-level, planning aimed at sustainability, which, in my view, we must not retreat from, as a genuine added value with which we wish to compete globally, supporting investments as already mentioned.

It has been stated on multiple occasions: new technologies, new processes, the upgrading of facilities for a European economy that effectively uses resources, including energy resources—indeed, starting with energy resources—and also the research into new and different materials.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, as we have recalled in this Chamber, next December the United Nations agency responsible for communication and information technologies will convene in Dubai for the World Conference on International Communications.
The subject is the renegotiation of international regulations on telecommunications.

We cannot forget, and I am pleased by the words of Commissioner Tajani regarding the work accomplished thus far, which cannot be called into question; nor can we allow the authority of the European Union to be bypassed.

a supranational organization such as the ITU, to which we know hundreds of states from around the world belong, many of which are known for issues related to democracy, transparency, and freedom of expression.
Therefore, it is not incorrect to consider that the pillars of the internet—freedom, participation, openness, innovation—are at risk.

Therefore, I believe it is necessary to safeguard the role of the Internet and the potential it especially holds for revitalizing economic growth, not merely that, but also for ensuring opportunities for all.
I therefore hope that the Commission works toward the goal that Europe at Dubai be united and speak with one voice.

Many controversial proposals have been presented at the negotiating table of the World Conference on International Telecommunications, particularly those suggesting changes to Internet governance, which undermine network neutrality and have negative implications for consumers.
Therefore, what is at risk is the freedom of the Internet, or more precisely, the freedom of expression and data protection.
Indeed, what some states have proposed would allow a government not only to intrude into the communications of its own citizens but even into the data passing through its own networks.
The leap from here to the limitation of freedom of expression to censorship is extremely short, and therefore I believe it is contradictory to the fundamental principles of the European Union.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, I have expressed my support for the colleague's text, particularly agreeing with the rapporteur in emphasizing the fundamental role played by national judges in applying the law within the Union.

I therefore consider it essential to fully support the European Union's efforts aimed at improving and coordinating training on jurisdiction for judicial and administrative authorities and legal professionals.
All initiatives in the sector should furthermore be intensified to fully realize the concept of a Union founded on the rule of law.

Mr. President, esteemed colleagues, in recent years, the extraction of so-called unconventional hydrocarbons, including shale gas and oil, has brought about an unprecedented transformation in global energy markets.
Europe, in order to address this situation and become less dependent on hydrocarbon imports, will therefore have to rely on its own potential gas and shale reserves.
In this regard, some Member States have already authorized shale gas exploration and are preparing for the extraction phase should discovery make it possible.
In such a context, it becomes necessary to monitor the regulations regarding shale gas extraction within the borders of the Union so that the effects of its extraction are more effective.
Extraction, undoubtedly economically viable, does not create environmental problems due to the potential release of harmful substances into groundwater or the atmosphere.
Mr. President, I believe today should be the opportunity to speak objectively about Kazakhstan and the significant agreement the country is negotiating with the European Union.
Kazakhstan is a crucial country for the stability and strategic future of Central Asia and is undoubtedly an important economic and commercial partner of the European Union.
It is a country that has already initiated a process of economic reform and also a democratic process, but still needs to make considerable efforts to fully complete these processes.

It is, however, a country that, with this objective, continues to demonstrate its readiness for new and stronger reforms, and I believe this is precisely the task that the European Union should undertake alongside them.

For this reason, I am certain that the signing of this enhanced agreement will enable progress, as its approval will strengthen an already solid and productive relationship and ensure continuous support from the European Union.

I remain convinced—and I believe this should have been emphasized even more in the report we will vote on tomorrow—that progress toward democracy and human rights must be accompanied by a renewed, open, and constructive mutual commitment.

Our objective must not be to adopt a negative stance toward these and other countries in the face of the failure to achieve immediate changes, but rather to support the efforts of these countries in realizing the goal, including the respect for the principles of human rights.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, colleagues, having thanked the rapporteurs of the reports we will vote on tomorrow, I would like to make just one brief remark regarding spinnage, and I fully associate myself with what I have heard tonight as a unanimous opinion expressed by all my colleagues.
but I wanted to focus on artisanal fishing, on the Ferrera report, which I greet and congratulate.

I wanted to say that, with the previous common fisheries policy, we have only succeeded in increasing the gap between the institutions governing fisheries and the sector's true actors, namely the fishermen.

Unfortunately, to date in Europe, the absence of a genuine definition of artisanal fishing has disadvantaged those who have always regarded fishing as an activity tied to the traditions of their region, their seas, and their cuisine.

Thus grew the conviction that the approach pursued by the European Commission aimed to standardize all fishing in Europe under large-scale industrial fishing, guided solely by market logic and disregarding the concerns of thousands of fishermen from a couple of...

Decades have operated according to rules related to ecosystem conservation, which I can only support, but which have never preserved the livelihood activities of the microeconomy of artisanal fishing.
This own-initiative report is a strong signal to the Commission and could translate into concrete actions benefiting the fishing microeconomy.
The report indeed highlights a number of advantages arising from allocating a minimum amount to small-scale fisheries from the European Fund.
Efforts must be made to promote integrated projects that benefit the coastal community as a whole, and, moreover, Commissioner, it will be essential to encourage young people's access to

We have discussed this sector several times in the Committee, because otherwise not only will fishing likely die out due to the depletion of seas and fish stocks, but it will also likely disappear because young people are no longer drawn to this field.
This report provides an opportunity to include training and motivational initiatives for young people who wish to enter the sector.
At this point, Mr. Toia, you have two minutes. As many colleagues have done today, I ask the Council, which has put forward this proposal: what exactly do you want to achieve, gentlemen of Europe?
Do you want to minimize it, freezing it in the future?
But it is the same Europe that subsequently seeks to relaunch integration through the Van Rompuy document.
But with what consistency, I wonder, is Van Rompuy handling these two files?

And what coherence can citizens rediscover between the decisions of July, employment and growth, the lofty appeals to hope and the future within the European Union that we hear even from the Council, and then this sudden plunge, in the name of realism, into a perspective of bare survival?
How will it be possible, with these figures and these cuts, to turn the much-praised major reforms of cohesion and the CAP into concrete actions and active policies?
These are sincere questions; we would like a clear, honest response, because there are here signs of institutional schizophrenia that Parliament no longer accepts, and that citizens no longer accept.
The Parliament wants consistency, it wants credibility.

The first criticism is that at the level of the budget, we have nothing but growth and investments; we are referring to the 2008 budget. It must be acknowledged that regarding heading one, Van Rompuy's proposal overturns the scandalous Cypriot assumption.
It is true that here the role of the budget as an indispensable driver of growth in certain sectors beyond national budgets is acknowledged.

But, clearly, growth and innovation must not come at the expense of other sectors, which are equally part of growth and jobs, such as the cohesion policy, and also, dear colleagues, the agricultural policy, which has boosted employment and is increasingly oriented toward innovation and the protection of the territory.
Growing Jobs is culture, training, transnational networks, the CEF, as well as solidarity and international development, human rights worldwide, and our contribution to the Millennium Goals.

Before us, however, unfortunately, is the usual spectacle of the Council—no vision, no leadership, a babel of conflicting and competing national interests, an inextricable knot of vetoes, expectations, and widespread dissatisfaction.
And Europe, unfortunately, is the great absentee.
Mr. President, shark finning is an environmentally unacceptable practice, as it threatens the sustainability of certain shark species that originate from those

on a social and economic level, as it involves the waste of a food commodity with commercial value, as well as from an ethical standpoint, as it demonstrates a complete lack of respect for animal life.

Although this practice has been prohibited since 2003 in waters and for the European fleet, the Commission has decided to submit a proposal aimed at eliminating the exemption allowing the removal of shark fins aboard Union fishing vessels.

This proposal, by further ensuring the protection that sharks require, will enable the European Union to actively contribute to the global effort underway to eradicate the cruel practice of shark finning in other parts of the world.
